Maintenance Regulations.clue: This FAA regulation sets the performance rules that a certificated repair station must follow.
Maintenance Regulations.explanation: Part 145 specifically governs certificated repair stations — their certification, personnel, facilities, and operational standards. Part 43 covers how maintenance is performed by anyone authorized; Part 91 covers aircraft operating rules; Part 65 covers airmen certificates. A common mistake is choosing Part 43, but Part 43 governs the work itself, not the repair station organization.
Maintenance Regulations.source: Identify the repair station performance regulation

Maintenance Regulations.clue: This FAA document certifies airworthiness approval and is used to trace the origin of an aircraft part or component.
Maintenance Regulations.explanation: Form 8130-3 is the Airworthiness Approval Tag. Form 337 documents major repairs and alterations. Form 8110-3 is a Statement of Compliance for TSO articles. Form 8060-4 is an Airman Certificate.
Maintenance Regulations.source: Explain the purpose of FAA Form 8130-3

Maintenance Regulations.clue: Per 14 CFR §43.9, this element is NOT required in a maintenance record entry.
Maintenance Regulations.explanation: §43.9 requires: description of work, date, name, certificate number, and return-to-service signature. The aircraft registration number is not a §43.9 required element — the five required elements describe who did the work and what was done, not the aircraft identifier.
Maintenance Regulations.source: State required elements of a maintenance record per §43.9

Maintenance Regulations.clue: These two regulations authorize appropriately rated repair stations to perform pitot-static and transponder inspections.
Maintenance Regulations.explanation: 91.411 covers altimeter/pitot-static system testing and 91.413 covers transponder testing. Both authorize appropriately rated repair stations. 91.207 covers ELTs. 91.171/177 cover VOR checks and minimum altitudes.
Maintenance Regulations.source: State which regulations authorize repair stations for pitot-static and transponder inspections

Maintenance Regulations.clue: Before performing safety-sensitive functions at a repair station with an FAA-approved drug program, a new hire must pass this.
Maintenance Regulations.explanation: FAA regulations require passing a pre-employment drug test before performing safety-sensitive maintenance functions when the station has an FAA-approved drug testing program. Background checks, practical exams, and probationary reviews are not the regulated requirement.
Maintenance Regulations.source: State the pre-employment drug test requirement for safety-sensitive functions

Maintenance Regulations.clue: Per 14 CFR Part 43, this information is required in a transponder test record but is most often omitted by technicians.
choices: The repair station's work order number
Maintenance Regulations.explanation: Part 43 requires the specific test equipment used to be documented — allowing verification that calibrated, appropriate equipment was actually used. Aircraft owner signature, flight hours, and work order numbers are not required transponder test record elements.
Maintenance Regulations.source: Document altimeter and transponder test results per Part 43

Maintenance Regulations.clue: In general aviation aircraft, mono audio is primarily used for these two applications.
choices: Flight director cues and GPWS alerts
Maintenance Regulations.explanation: GA audio systems use mono (single-channel) audio for intercom communications and speaker monitoring. Stereo separation provides no operational benefit for ATC communications or crew intercom — the same audio signal goes to all headsets and speakers.
Maintenance Regulations.source: State the typical use of mono audio in GA aircraft

Maintenance Regulations.clue: In a Part 145 shop, cluttered walkways are dangerous primarily because of these three hazards.
choices: Fuel contamination, EMI, and oil spills
FOD, trip hazards, and tool loss
Maintenance Regulations.explanation: Clutter creates FOD risk (debris entering aircraft), trip hazards for personnel, and tool accountability failures. These are the core reasons for mandatory housekeeping standards in Part 145 facilities.
Maintenance Regulations.source: Explain why clean work areas are critical in a Part 145 shop

Maintenance Regulations.clue: Per 14 CFR Part 43 Appendix A, this type of change to type design might appreciably affect structural strength, flight characteristics, or airworthiness.
Maintenance Regulations.explanation: A major alteration is a type design change that might appreciably affect structural strength, flight characteristics, or other airworthiness qualities — requiring approved data such as an STC or Form 337.
Maintenance Regulations.source: State the definition of a major alteration per Part 43 Appendix A

Maintenance Regulations.clue: Even if a radio is on the repair station's capability list, maintenance cannot legally proceed without this being available in the shop.
choices: The current revision of the manufacturer's maintenance manual
Maintenance Regulations.explanation: Capability list listing provides authorization, but Part 145 also requires current technical data. An outdated manual does not satisfy this requirement — authorization and current data are both required, not interchangeable.
Maintenance Regulations.source: Determine when maintenance cannot proceed due to missing current technical data

Maintenance Regulations.clue: The FAA definition of 'maintenance' includes inspection, overhaul, repair, preservation, and parts replacement — but explicitly excludes this.
Maintenance Regulations.explanation: The FAA defines maintenance as inspection, overhaul, repair, preservation, and parts replacement. Preventive maintenance is a separate regulatory category limited to certificated pilots on aircraft they own or operate — explicitly excluded from the definition of maintenance.
Maintenance Regulations.source: State the FAA definition of maintenance

Maintenance Regulations.clue: When troubleshooting an intermittent NAV flag on a VOR receiver in a Part 145 shop, this is the required first action before replacing any components.
choices: Swap the receiver with a known-good unit to isolate the LRU
Pull the circuit breaker and reset the system
Check the antenna coax cable for continuity
Maintenance Regulations.explanation: Part 145 requires maintenance to be performed per approved technical data. Consulting the maintenance manual is the mandatory first step — it defines the approved diagnostic procedure. Swapping components without approved data is shotgun troubleshooting and a regulatory violation.
Maintenance Regulations.source: Apply the correct first step when troubleshooting in a Part 145 shop

Maintenance Regulations.clue: An STC installation includes this document, which classifies the installation as a major alteration regardless of the part's TSO status.
Maintenance Regulations.explanation: A flight manual supplement changes the aircraft's approved operating limitations or procedures — that change to type design qualifies the installation as a major alteration under Part 43 Appendix A, regardless of the part's TSO approval.
Maintenance Regulations.source: Classify STC installations with flight manual supplements as major alterations

Maintenance Regulations.clue: This regulation governs performance rules for aircraft maintenance, preventive maintenance, and alterations — applying to any authorized person, not just repair stations.
Maintenance Regulations.explanation: Part 43 sets the standards for how maintenance work must be performed and applies broadly — to individual mechanics, repair stations, and other authorized persons. Part 145 governs the repair station organization. Part 43 governs the work itself.
Maintenance Regulations.source: State that 14 CFR Part 43 governs maintenance performance rules

Maintenance Regulations.clue: When inserting or extracting terminated wires from standard circular connectors, using these prevents pin damage and difficult-to-diagnose intermittent faults.
choices: Proper manufacturer-specified insertion and extraction tools
A heat gun and connector pick
Maintenance Regulations.explanation: Manufacturer-specified insertion and extraction tools are required. Improvised tools damage pins, bend contacts, or crack the connector insert — creating intermittent faults that are extremely difficult to diagnose later.
Maintenance Regulations.source: Apply correct insertion/extraction tool practice for circular connectors

Maintenance Regulations.clue: Replacing a TSO part with another TSO part of the same type, done per approved data, is generally not classified as this.
Maintenance Regulations.explanation: TSO-for-TSO replacement of the same type per approved data is not a major alteration because it does not appreciably change the aircraft's airworthiness qualities. The two key qualifiers are 'same type' and 'per approved data.'
Maintenance Regulations.source: Determine that TSO-for-TSO replacement per approved data is not a major alteration

Maintenance Regulations.clue: When installing avionics wiring in a Part 145 shop, this advisory circular — or the aircraft manufacturer's wiring data — is the primary standard to follow.
Maintenance Regulations.explanation: AC 43.13-1B Chapter 11 covers aircraft electrical wiring acceptable methods. When manufacturer wiring data exists it takes precedence, but AC 43.13-1B is the primary fallback. AC 20-165B covers ADS-B. AC 91-78 covers EFBs. AC 43.13-2B covers aircraft structures.
Maintenance Regulations.source: State the primary wiring standard for avionics installation in Part 145

Maintenance Regulations.clue: Before using specialized test equipment in a Part 145 shop, a technician must verify that this is current and within its interval.
Maintenance Regulations.explanation: All precision test equipment must have a current calibration label. Using out-of-calibration equipment invalidates measurements and may produce legally non-compliant maintenance records.
Maintenance Regulations.source: Verify test equipment calibration before use in a Part 145 shop

Maintenance Regulations.clue: Of these log entry elements, this one is required by §43.9 but is most often omitted by new technicians.
Maintenance Regulations.explanation: The technician's certificate number is most frequently omitted — new techs include their name and signature but forget the certificate number, which §43.9 explicitly requires and which ties the entry to a specific FAA-certificated individual.
Maintenance Regulations.source: List required elements of a maintenance log entry for an avionics repair

Maintenance Regulations.clue: Per 14 CFR 91.413, transponder systems must be tested and inspected at this interval.
Maintenance Regulations.explanation: 91.413 requires transponder testing every 24 calendar months — not 12 months or flight hours. The interval is calendar-based, running from the date of the last check regardless of hours flown.
Maintenance Regulations.source: State that 91.413 requires transponder testing every 24 calendar months

Maintenance Regulations.clue: A Part 145 repair station's authorized scope of work is defined and limited by these, listed in its operations specifications.
choices: Its ratings and classes in the Ops Specs
Maintenance Regulations.explanation: Operations specifications define what a Part 145 station is authorized to do. Individual A&P certificates authorize the individual technician, not the shop organization. ISO certification is a quality management standard, not an FAA authorization.
Maintenance Regulations.source: Explain that Part 145 shop work is limited to its specific ratings and classes

Maintenance Regulations.clue: This is the specific regulation number requiring periodic testing and inspection of transponder systems.
Maintenance Regulations.explanation: 91.413 is specifically for transponders. 91.411 covers altimeter and pitot-static systems. 91.207 covers ELTs. Part 43 covers maintenance standards generally.
Maintenance Regulations.source: State the specific regulation requiring periodic transponder testing

Maintenance Regulations.clue: This FAA approval permits modifying a type-certificated aircraft in a way not covered by the original type certificate.
Maintenance Regulations.explanation: An STC is the FAA approval for modifications beyond the original type certificate. An AD mandates a safety change. Form 337 documents a major repair or alteration. A PMA approves a replacement part. Only an STC authorizes the modification itself.
Maintenance Regulations.source: State the definition of an STC

Maintenance Regulations.clue: Under Part 145, these two roles share responsibility for ensuring current technical data is available and used during maintenance.
choices: The Quality Control department and accountable manager
The FAA FSDO and repair station owner
The technician and their direct supervisor
Maintenance Regulations.explanation: Part 145 places responsibility for current technical data on the Quality Control system and the accountable manager. QC ensures data is current and available. The accountable manager is the person the FAA holds responsible for the station's overall compliance.
Maintenance Regulations.source: State who is responsible for ensuring current technical data availability under Part 145

Maintenance Regulations.clue: Under 14 CFR Part 43, this type of maintenance is limited to certificated pilots on aircraft they own or operate, for simple listed tasks only.
Maintenance Regulations.explanation: Preventive maintenance is the only maintenance a certificated pilot (not a mechanic) may legally perform, and only on aircraft they own or operate — limited to simple tasks listed in Part 43 Appendix A.
Maintenance Regulations.source: Explain that preventive maintenance under Part 43 is limited to certificated pilots on aircraft they own or operate

Maintenance Regulations.clue: Most Part 145 repair station manuals require personnel to complete recurrent training at least this often.
Maintenance Regulations.explanation: The industry standard in Part 145 repair station manuals is recurrent training every 24 months — aligning with other 24-month aviation maintenance cycles. The specific interval is set in each station's manual; 24 months is the standard most stations use.
Maintenance Regulations.source: State the typical recurrent training interval in Part 145 station manuals

Maintenance Regulations.clue: This class of fire involves energized electrical equipment and requires non-conductive extinguishing agents.
Maintenance Regulations.explanation: Class C = energized electrical equipment. Class A = ordinary combustibles. Class B = flammable liquids and gases. Class D = combustible metals. Using water on a Class C fire creates an electrocution hazard.
Maintenance Regulations.source: State that Class C fires involve energized electrical equipment

Maintenance Regulations.clue: A pilot hears a constant tone on the frequency instead of ATC responses. This is the most likely diagnosis.
choices: ILS localizer signal interference on the comm frequency
Maintenance Regulations.explanation: A constant tone replacing all voice traffic is the classic stuck PTT symptom. Someone's microphone is transmitting continuously and blocking the frequency. Squelch failure causes noise, not a tone. ATC transmitter failure causes silence. ILS bleed-over affects specific frequencies differently.
Maintenance Regulations.source: Diagnose a stuck microphone as the cause of a constant tone on a comm frequency

Maintenance Regulations.clue: To confirm a switch is causing a parasitic draw, you place this instrument in series with the circuit and activate the switch.
Maintenance Regulations.explanation: Parasitic draw is current — measured with an ammeter in series. A voltmeter measures voltage in parallel. An ohmmeter measures resistance with power off. A wattmeter measures power but is not the standard tool for parasitic draw diagnosis.
Maintenance Regulations.source: Confirm a parasitic draw source using ammeter in series

Maintenance Regulations.clue: This document must be readily available for each hazardous material used in a Part 145 avionics shop.
Maintenance Regulations.explanation: OSHA requires Safety Data Sheets (SDS) to be readily accessible for all hazardous materials in the workplace. In an avionics shop, this includes solvents, adhesives, coatings, and cleaning agents.
Maintenance Regulations.source: State that an SDS must be available for each hazardous material in a Part 145 shop

Maintenance Regulations.clue: This is the primary reason hearing protection is critical in a busy avionics shop with engine run-up activity nearby.
choices: Short-term hearing loss affects radio communication quality during operations
OSHA requires it only for insurance liability compliance purposes
Sustained exposure causes cumulative permanent noise-induced hearing loss
Engine noise creates EMI that interferes with electronic test equipment
Maintenance Regulations.explanation: Noise-induced hearing loss is cumulative and permanent — each unprotected exposure contributes to total damage even if no single event seems severe. It is a long-term occupational hazard, not just an acute discomfort issue.
Maintenance Regulations.source: Explain why hearing protection is critical in a busy avionics shop

Maintenance Regulations.clue: After completing a 91.411 pitot-static test, the maintenance record must include the specific test equipment used — including this detail about each instrument, which proves the measurements were valid.
choices: The equipment's original purchase date and vendor
The calibration due date, confirming the equipment was in calibration at the time of the test
The equipment's serial number and firmware version
The equipment manufacturer's ISO 9001 certification number

Maintenance Regulations.clue: A technician signs off an avionics installation in the aircraft logbook but omits this specific §43.9 element — which is the single most common regulatory deficiency found during FSDO surveillance of Part 145 shops.

A technician discovers that a previous maintenance record entry references the wrong part number for an installed component. Under Part 43, the correct action is this — not simply crossing it out or adding a note.
choices: Make a new corrective entry referencing the original entry, with the correct part number and a new signature
White out the incorrect number and write the correct one with initials and date
Contact the FAA FSDO to request a formal records amendment
Remove the page from the logbook and replace it with a corrected version

Maintenance Regulations.clue: Per 14 CFR 91.411, altimeter and pitot-static system inspections must be performed at this fixed interval — the same interval required for transponder checks under 91.413.

During a transponder test per Part 43 Appendix F, the test set verifies reply frequency, power output, suppression, and this parameter — which confirms the transponder correctly reports the aircraft's barometric altitude.
choices: Mode C altitude encoding accuracy within ±125 feet
ATC radar interrogation response delay within 3 microseconds
Squawk code assignment timing within 10 milliseconds

Maintenance Regulations.clue: An ELT inspection per 91.207 requires verifying battery expiration, proper mounting, antenna condition, and this — which must be performed only during the first five minutes of each hour to avoid triggering a false alert to search-and-rescue satellites.
choices: A full-duration 60-second transmission test on 406 MHz
A brief operational test limited to three audible sweeps on 121.5 MHz
A 30-second transmission test monitored on the aircraft's comm radio
No transmission test — only a visual inspection of the cockpit-mounted status light

Basic Electrical.clue: This formula expresses current as voltage divided by resistance and is the foundational equation of DC circuit analysis.
Basic Electrical.explanation: I = V / R is Ohm's Law solved for current. Rearranging gives the other two forms: V = I x R (solve for voltage) and R = V / I (solve for resistance). All three rearrangements are equally valid — knowing which variable is unknown determines which form to use.
Basic Electrical.source: State Ohm's Law: I = V / R

Basic Electrical.clue: Applying Ohm's Law to a 24-volt circuit with 12 ohms of total resistance produces this current value.
Basic Electrical.explanation: I = V / R = 24V / 12Ω = 2 amps. Dividing 24 by 12 gives exactly 2. The distractor 0.5 amps results from inverting (12/24). The distractor 12 amps ignores the voltage entirely. The distractor 288 amps multiplies instead of divides.
Basic Electrical.source: Calculate current: 24V / 12 ohms = 2A

Basic Electrical.clue: This formula gives the total resistance of a series circuit containing three resistors: Rt equals R1 plus R2 plus this.
choices: R1 + R2 — the third resistor does not add resistance
Basic Electrical.explanation: In a series circuit, Rt = R1 + R2 + R3. The formula completes with R3 because every series resistor contributes its full value to total opposition. Unlike parallel circuits, series resistors simply add together with no complex reciprocal calculation needed.
Basic Electrical.source: Calculate total series resistance: Rt = R1 + R2 + R3

Basic Electrical.clue: Navigation light bulbs are wired this way so that the failure of one lamp does not extinguish the others.
choices: In parallel, each bulb on its own independent branch
In series, sharing a single current path
In series-parallel with a bypass switch
Through a single common resistor to limit current
Basic Electrical.explanation: Parallel wiring gives each bulb an independent path to the bus. When one bulb burns out (open circuit), that branch is lost but all other branches remain unaffected. In a series circuit, one open stops all current and all lights would fail — clearly unacceptable for a safety-critical system.
Basic Electrical.source: Analyze parallel circuit: remaining loads stay lit if one fails

Basic Electrical.clue: If the voltage applied to a fixed-resistance circuit is doubled, this is what happens to the current.
Basic Electrical.explanation: I = V / R. If V doubles and R is unchanged, the ratio V/R doubles. Current and voltage are directly proportional when resistance is constant. This is a linear relationship — a 2x change in voltage produces a 2x change in current, not a squared or square-root change.
Basic Electrical.source: Predict current doubles when voltage doubles (R constant)

Basic Electrical.clue: A component with 14 volts across it and 7 amps through it has this resistance value.
Basic Electrical.explanation: R = V / I = 14V / 7A = 2 ohms. The distractor 0.5 ohms results from inverting (7/14). The distractor 7 ohms confuses resistance with current value. The distractor 98 ohms multiplies instead of divides. Always divide voltage by current to find resistance.
Basic Electrical.source: Calculate resistance: 14V / 7A = 2 ohms

Basic Electrical.clue: A 12-volt circuit carrying 2 amperes dissipates this amount of power.
Basic Electrical.explanation: P = V x I = 12V x 2A = 24 watts. The distractor 6 watts comes from dividing. The distractor 14 watts adds instead of multiplies. The distractor 144 watts squares the voltage. Power is always voltage times current — not a sum, quotient, or square.
Basic Electrical.source: Calculate power: P = V x I, 12V x 2A = 24W

Basic Electrical.clue: When three parallel branches draw 1A, 2A, and 3A respectively, this formula and result give the total current from the source.
choices: It = I1 x I2 x I3 = 6A
It = the largest branch current only = 3A
Basic Electrical.explanation: Total parallel current is the sum of all branch currents: It = 1 + 2 + 3 = 6A. Unlike series circuits where current is the same everywhere, parallel circuits split current among branches — but the source must supply all branches combined. This is why adding parallel loads increases total current draw.
Basic Electrical.source: Calculate total parallel current: It = I1 + I2 + I3

Basic Electrical.clue: A 28-volt DC bus supplying 25 amps to the combined avionics load delivers this total power.
Basic Electrical.explanation: P = V x I = 28 x 25 = 700 watts. The 28VDC aircraft bus is a standard reference point for power calculations. Knowing total power draw — not just individual loads — is required when verifying the aircraft's alternator capacity is sufficient for all installed equipment.
Basic Electrical.source: Calculate power on 28V bus at 25A = 700W

Basic Electrical.clue: This form of Ohm's Law is used to calculate voltage when current and resistance are both known.
Basic Electrical.explanation: V = I x R is Ohm's Law solved for voltage. It shows that voltage equals the product of current and resistance. The other options are incorrect rearrangements: dividing by R gives current, not voltage. This form is used to calculate voltage drop across a resistor when current and resistance are known.
Basic Electrical.source: State Ohm's Law as V = I x R

Basic Electrical.clue: If current through a fixed resistor doubles, this is the effect on the voltage across that resistor.
Basic Electrical.explanation: V = I x R. If I doubles and R is constant, the product I x R doubles, so V doubles. Voltage and current are directly proportional for a given resistance — a linear relationship. This is different from power, which scales with the square of current.
Basic Electrical.source: Predict voltage doubles when current doubles (R constant)

Basic Electrical.clue: This is the effect on total circuit resistance each time a new load is added in parallel across the aircraft bus.
choices: Total resistance increases because more components are added
Total resistance stays the same because each branch is independent
Total resistance is undefined until all branch values are known
Total resistance decreases because additional current paths are created
Basic Electrical.explanation: Every parallel branch creates an additional path for current, so total resistance drops. The formula 1/Rt = 1/R1 + 1/R2 + ... always produces a result smaller than the smallest individual branch. This is why adding avionics loads to a bus increases total current draw — lower total resistance draws more current from the same voltage.
Basic Electrical.source: Understand adding parallel resistors reduces total resistance

Basic Electrical.clue: When voltage doubles and current doubles simultaneously in a circuit, power changes by this factor.
choices: It doubles — because two quantities each doubled
It quadruples — because 2V x 2I = 4 x original power
It triples — the midpoint between doubling each quantity
It stays the same — voltage and current increases cancel out
Basic Electrical.explanation: P = V x I. If V becomes 2V and I becomes 2I, then new power = 2V x 2I = 4 x (V x I). Power is the product of voltage and current — when both factors double, the product increases by 2 x 2 = 4. This compounding effect is why fault conditions with both elevated voltage and current can cause catastrophic damage rapidly.
Basic Electrical.source: Predict power quadruples when both V and I double

Basic Electrical.clue: The parallel combination of 10,000 ohms, 1,000 ohms, and 500 ohms produces this total resistance.
Basic Electrical.explanation: 1/Rt = 1/10000 + 1/1000 + 1/500 = 0.0001 + 0.001 + 0.002 = 0.0021. Rt = 1/0.0021 ≈ 476 ohms. The answer must be less than 500 ohms (the smallest branch). Adding 11,500 (series) or averaging (3,833) are common errors from misapplying the formula.
Basic Electrical.source: Calculate parallel resistance: 10k, 1k, 500 ohms ≈ 476 ohms

Basic Electrical.clue: A 12-volt battery connected to a 6-ohm circuit drives this much current.
Basic Electrical.explanation: I = V / R = 12V / 6Ω = 2 amps. The distractor 0.5 amps comes from inverting (6/12). The distractor 6 amps ignores the voltage. The distractor 72 amps multiplies voltage by resistance. Always divide voltage by resistance to find current.
Basic Electrical.source: Calculate current: 12V / 6 ohms = 2A

Basic Electrical.clue: With voltage held constant, doubling the resistance in a circuit produces this change in current.
Basic Electrical.explanation: I = V / R. If R doubles (becomes 2R) and V is constant: new current = V / (2R) = (V/R) / 2 = half the original current. Resistance and current are inversely proportional at constant voltage — a fundamental inverse relationship in Ohm's Law.
Basic Electrical.source: Predict current halved when resistance doubles (V constant)

Basic Electrical.clue: These two instruments are the correct choices for verifying the output frequency of an aircraft AC alternator during bench testing.
choices: A frequency counter or calibrated oscilloscope
A megohmmeter and a clamp meter
A digital multimeter and a tachometer
Basic Electrical.explanation: A frequency counter directly measures cycles per second (Hz). A calibrated oscilloscope allows a technician to measure the period of one cycle and calculate frequency from it. A standard DMM's AC voltage function measures RMS magnitude, not frequency — it will not confirm the alternator is outputting 400 Hz versus 380 Hz or 420 Hz.
Basic Electrical.source: Select correct instrument to verify AC alternator output frequency

Basic Electrical.clue: The unit used to measure the number of complete AC cycles occurring each second in an electrical system.
Basic Electrical.explanation: Hertz (Hz) is the SI unit of frequency, named after Heinrich Hertz. One hertz equals one complete cycle per second. Aircraft AC systems operate at 400 Hz (400 cycles per second), while US household power is 60 Hz. Frequency is distinct from voltage (V), resistance (Ω), or power (W).
Basic Electrical.source: State that frequency is measured in hertz (Hz)

Basic Electrical.clue: This is the SI unit of capacitance, though avionics capacitors are almost always specified in one-millionth of this unit.
Basic Electrical.explanation: The farad (F) is the SI unit of capacitance. Because one farad is impractically large, avionics capacitors are specified in microfarads (µF = 10⁻⁶ F) or picofarads (pF = 10⁻¹² F). The henry measures inductance, the ohm measures resistance, and the siemen measures conductance.
Basic Electrical.source: State that capacitance is measured in farads; microfarads most common in avionics

Basic Electrical.clue: This is the shape of the voltage waveform produced by an AC generator, characterized by smooth, continuous alternation above and below zero.
choices: A sawtooth wave with sharp transitions
A square wave alternating between fixed positive and negative values
A sinusoidal curve that smoothly crosses zero at the system frequency
A pulsed DC waveform that never goes below zero
Basic Electrical.explanation: AC generators produce a sinusoidal waveform because the magnetic flux through the rotating winding follows a sine function. The voltage rises smoothly to a positive peak, crosses zero, falls to a negative peak, and returns to zero each cycle. Square waves and sawtooth waves are generated by electronic circuits, not rotating AC alternators.
Basic Electrical.source: Describe AC waveform as sinusoidal curve alternating above/below zero

Basic Electrical.clue: This is the primary advantage of AC electrical systems over DC in aircraft: the ability to use this device to change voltage levels and reduce wiring weight.
choices: Rectifiers, which convert AC to DC for lightweight storage
Alternators, which produce AC more efficiently than DC generators
Transformers, which step voltage up or down enabling lighter wiring
Inverters, which convert DC to AC for high-voltage loads
Basic Electrical.explanation: Transformers only work with AC — they cannot change DC voltage levels. By stepping up voltage, transformers allow the same power to be transmitted at lower current. Lower current means smaller wire is adequate (P = V x I; at higher V, I is lower for the same P), reducing wire bundle weight. This is why aircraft with high power loads (radar, galleys) use AC distribution.
Basic Electrical.source: State AC advantage: transformers enable lighter wiring

Basic Electrical.clue: This property of inductors opposes AC current flow, is measured in ohms, and increases as the AC frequency rises.
Basic Electrical.explanation: Inductive reactance (XL = 2πfL) describes how strongly an inductor opposes AC current. It is measured in ohms, like resistance, but is frequency-dependent. As frequency increases, XL increases — the opposite of capacitive reactance (XC), which decreases with frequency. At DC (f = 0), an ideal inductor has zero reactance and acts as a plain wire.
Basic Electrical.source: State inductive reactance: opposition to AC by inductors, measured in ohms, increases with frequency

Basic Electrical.clue: This AC component transfers power between two circuits through electromagnetic induction while changing the voltage level according to its turns ratio.
choices: A rectifier, which converts AC to DC
A relay, which uses magnetic force to switch contacts
A capacitor, which stores energy in an electric field
A transformer, which steps voltage up or down via electromagnetic induction
Basic Electrical.explanation: A transformer uses a changing magnetic field (created by AC in the primary winding) to induce voltage in the secondary winding. The ratio of turns determines whether voltage is stepped up or down. A rectifier converts AC to DC. A relay uses magnetism to switch, not to transfer power between windings. A capacitor stores energy electrostatically, not electromagnetically.
Basic Electrical.source: Explain how transformers step voltage via electromagnetic induction

Basic Electrical.clue: This instrument is chosen over a standard DMM when measuring the resistance of aircraft bonding straps because it has far greater precision at sub-ohm values.
Basic Electrical.explanation: A milliohmmeter measures resistance in the milliohm range with high accuracy. Standard DMMs lack the resolution to reliably measure bonding resistance, which must typically be under 0.003 ohms per AC 43.13.
Basic Electrical.source: Select milliohmmeter for bonding resistance measurement

Basic Electrical.clue: Bonding straps, connector contact resistance, and ground return paths are the primary targets of this precision low-resistance instrument.
Basic Electrical.explanation: The milliohmmeter's primary applications are bonding straps (structural ground integrity), connector contacts (ensuring good electrical connections), and ground return paths (verifying current return to bus).
Basic Electrical.source: State primary use of milliohmmeter

Basic Electrical.clue: When using a multimeter to measure voltage across a component, this is how the meter must be connected and what the circuit state must be.
Basic Electrical.explanation: Voltage measurement requires parallel connection (across the component) with the circuit powered on. Series connection would interrupt the circuit; circuit off would give zero volts.
Basic Electrical.source: Connect multimeter in parallel to measure voltage

Basic Electrical.clue: Faulty relay contacts, stuck switches, and incorrect avionics installation wiring are the three common causes of this battery-draining phenomenon.
Basic Electrical.explanation: Parasitic draw is current consumed when the master is OFF. Its common causes are faulty relay contacts (stuck closed), stuck switches (not opening), and incorrectly wired avionics that remain powered after shutdown.
Basic Electrical.source: List causes of parasitic current draw

Basic Electrical.clue: A milliohmmeter gives a fluctuating reading on a bonding strap. This is the correct next step before re-measuring.
choices: Increase torque on the bonding fastener only
Inspect and clean both attachment points for corrosion
Basic Electrical.explanation: Fluctuating readings indicate an intermittent contact, almost always due to corrosion at the attachment surface. Clean both surfaces, reinstall, and re-measure. Simply retorquing without cleaning doesn't address the corrosion.
Basic Electrical.source: Diagnose fluctuating milliohmmeter reading on bonding strap

Basic Electrical.clue: Avionics voltage is fluctuating but remains within limits. This intermittent ground connection or failing component is the most likely cause.
choices: Intermittent ground connection or failing voltage regulator
Open circuit in the main bus
Basic Electrical.explanation: Unstable but within-limits voltage points to intermittent (not failed) components. An intermittent ground adds variable resistance to the return path; a failing regulator produces inconsistent output — both create fluctuating readings.
Basic Electrical.source: Diagnose unstable within-limits voltage

Basic Electrical.clue: Voltage measured across a load is lower than the supply voltage would predict. Corroded connections and undersized wiring are examples of this root cause.
choices: Excessive current draw by the load
Excessive resistance between source and load
Basic Electrical.explanation: Excessive resistance in the wiring or connectors creates a voltage drop (V=IR) before reaching the load. Corroded connections and undersized wiring are the most common culprits. A true open circuit would give zero volts, not reduced volts.
Basic Electrical.source: Diagnose lower-than-expected voltage across load

Basic Electrical.clue: Before connecting a multimeter to measure resistance, you must do these two things to protect the meter and get accurate readings.
choices: Set the range to auto and enable AC mode
Verify calibration and enable DC mode
Connect in series and set to the highest range
Turn the circuit power off and discharge capacitors
Basic Electrical.explanation: Measuring resistance with power applied will damage the meter's ohmmeter circuit and give false readings. Capacitors must also be discharged — they can hold charge that appears as voltage to the meter.
Basic Electrical.source: Correct procedure before measuring resistance with multimeter

Basic Electrical.clue: A 0.5V drop measured in a 24V DC circuit between the source and a test point indicates this is present in the wiring or connectors along that path.
choices: Resistance in the wiring or connectors
Basic Electrical.explanation: Voltage drop = I × R. Any resistance in the wiring path (corroded pin, undersized wire, loose terminal) creates a proportional voltage drop. A 0.5V drop in a 24V circuit indicates measurable resistance between the source and test point.
Basic Electrical.source: Identify cause of 0.5V drop in 24V DC circuit

Basic Electrical.clue: A voltmeter reads 0V across a component in an energized circuit. This upstream condition — blown fuse, tripped breaker, or broken wire — is the most likely explanation.
choices: Open circuit upstream of the component
Basic Electrical.explanation: 0V across a component in a live circuit means no current is flowing through it — which requires an open circuit upstream. Check fuses, breakers, and wire continuity between the source and the component.
Basic Electrical.source: Diagnose 0V across component in active circuit

Basic Electrical.clue: This instrument applies high voltage to test the insulation resistance of wiring, motors, and generator windings, measuring results in megohms.
Basic Electrical.explanation: The megohmmeter (megger) applies high DC voltage to stress insulation and measure its resistance in megohms. Low readings indicate degraded or compromised insulation that could lead to shorts or failures.
Basic Electrical.source: Identify megohmmeter use for insulation resistance testing

Basic Electrical.clue: Current consumed by a circuit when the aircraft's master switch is OFF, which drains the battery over time, is called this.
Basic Electrical.explanation: Parasitic current draw is any current flowing when the master is off. It slowly discharges the battery, leading to a dead battery after the aircraft sits unused. Common in aircraft with improperly installed avionics or failing relays.
Basic Electrical.source: Define parasitic current draw

Basic Electrical.clue: Aircraft lighting circuit bulbs that are dimmer than normal indicate this condition in the wiring or connectors between the bus and the bulbs.
choices: Excessive resistance causing a voltage drop
An open circuit interrupting current flow
A short circuit bypassing the bulbs
Basic Electrical.explanation: Dim bulbs mean reduced voltage at the bulb (V_bulb = V_supply − V_drop). Excessive resistance in the wiring or connectors upstream creates a voltage drop, leaving less voltage for the bulb — resulting in reduced brightness.
Basic Electrical.source: Diagnose dimmer-than-normal bulbs

Basic Electrical.clue: A 0.5V drop measured in a 24V circuit can be caused by resistance in any of these three places in the circuit path.
choices: The bus, the alternator, or the battery
The relay, the switch, or the fuse
The wiring, the connectors, or the ground return path
The LRU, the bus bar, or the voltage regulator
Basic Electrical.explanation: Voltage drop (V=IR) can occur anywhere current flows: supply wiring, connector pins, and the ground return path. Technicians often forget to check the ground return, which can have just as much resistance as the supply side.
Basic Electrical.source: Identify cause of 0.5V drop — wiring, connectors, or ground

Basic Electrical.clue: The pitot heat circuit breaker trips the instant it is reset. This is the correct next action before any further steps.
choices: Do not reset again; isolate the circuit and inspect for a short or failed element
Replace the circuit breaker with a higher-rated one
Reset it a second time to confirm the fault
Apply power to the pitot heat and measure current draw
Basic Electrical.explanation: An immediately-tripping breaker signals a hard fault — likely a short or failed heating element. Repeated resets force excessive current through the fault, risking fire or further damage. Isolate the circuit, then inspect before any power is reapplied.
Basic Electrical.source: Diagnose breaker that trips immediately on reset

Basic Electrical.clue: When a newly installed avionics system fails to power on, these are the first three things to verify before any further troubleshooting.
choices: Software version, antenna connection, and cooling airflow
Connector torque, backshell install, and wire gauge verification
Frequency programming, squelch level, and display brightness
Circuit breaker set, correct bus connection, and proper ground termination
Basic Electrical.explanation: New installation no-power faults are almost always basics: breaker not set (or tripped), power wire on wrong bus, or ground wire not properly terminated. Check these three before any deeper troubleshooting.
Basic Electrical.source: First checks when new avionics won't power up

Basic Electrical.clue: To measure current in a circuit, the ammeter must be connected this way, requiring the circuit to be broken to insert it.
choices: In series, circuit powered off to insert, then powered on
Basic Electrical.explanation: Current measurement requires series connection — break the circuit, insert the ammeter, then restore power so current flows through the meter. Parallel connection would short the supply through the meter's near-zero resistance.
Basic Electrical.source: Connect ammeter in series to measure current

Basic Electrical.clue: These two advantages over generators explain why modern DC aircraft electrical systems use alternators as the primary power source.
choices: Higher voltage output and simpler brush design
Lower RPM usable output and lighter weight
AC output capability and lower cost
Solid-state rectification and no field winding
Basic Electrical.explanation: Alternators produce usable output at low RPM (charging at idle) and are lighter than equivalent generators. These two factors make them the standard in modern GA and transport aircraft DC electrical systems.
Basic Electrical.source: Explain why alternators are preferred over generators

Basic Electrical.clue: These protective devices are sized to the wire's current capacity and interrupt the circuit before wiring overheats, primarily protecting against this hazard.
choices: Overcurrent conditions that could cause fire or damage
Basic Electrical.explanation: Fuses and circuit breakers are overcurrent protection devices. They interrupt the circuit when current exceeds the wire's safe capacity, preventing the wiring from overheating and catching fire. They are sized to protect the wire, not necessarily the connected equipment.
Basic Electrical.source: Explain purpose of fuses and circuit breakers

Basic Electrical.clue: This component serves as the initial power source in a DC aircraft electrical system, providing energy for engine starting before the alternator comes online.
Basic Electrical.explanation: The battery provides initial power for engine starting and pre-start avionics. Once the engine reaches sufficient RPM, the alternator takes over and recharges the battery. Without battery power, engine starting is not possible.
Basic Electrical.source: State battery role as initial power source

Basic Electrical.clue: This protective device opens the circuit permanently when current exceeds its rated value, protecting wiring from heat and fire, and must be replaced after operating.
Basic Electrical.explanation: A fuse permanently opens when its element melts due to overcurrent. Unlike a circuit breaker (which can be reset), a blown fuse must be replaced. Both protect wiring from current levels that would cause overheating.
Basic Electrical.source: Explain fuse purpose — open circuit on overcurrent

Basic Electrical.clue: This component maintains consistent DC output voltage from an alternator or generator despite changes in engine RPM and electrical load.
Basic Electrical.explanation: The voltage regulator controls the alternator's field current to hold output voltage steady — typically 27.5–28.5V — regardless of engine speed or how many electrical loads are active. Without it, voltage would fluctuate widely.
Basic Electrical.source: Identify voltage regulator function

Basic Electrical.clue: When disconnecting an aircraft battery, this cable must be removed first to prevent sparking and accidental short circuits.
Basic Electrical.explanation: Remove the negative (ground) cable first. With the negative disconnected, accidental contact of a tool with the airframe while removing the positive cable won't complete a circuit — no spark, no short. Removing positive first leaves the battery connected to the airframe, creating shock and spark hazard.
Basic Electrical.source: State correct first step when disconnecting aircraft battery

Basic Electrical.clue: Avionics, lights, ignition systems, and fuel pumps are examples of equipment powered by this aircraft system during all phases of operation.
Basic Electrical.explanation: The DC electrical system powers all electrically-dependent equipment throughout the aircraft. Failure of this system in flight can result in loss of communications, navigation, engine management (fuel pump), and lighting.
Basic Electrical.source: Explain primary purpose of aircraft DC electrical system

Basic Electrical.clue: This common distribution point in an aircraft electrical system receives power from one or more sources and supplies it to multiple electrical loads simultaneously.
Basic Electrical.explanation: An electrical bus is the distribution backbone of the aircraft electrical system — it connects power sources (battery, alternator) to all loads (avionics, lights, systems) through a common node. Multiple buses (avionics bus, essential bus, main bus) provide load separation and redundancy.
Basic Electrical.source: Define aircraft electrical bus

Basic Electrical.clue: These two components — one that shunts high-frequency noise to ground and one that blocks it from passing — are used together to filter alternator noise from the aircraft electrical bus.
Basic Electrical.explanation: Capacitors shunt high-frequency alternator noise to ground; inductors (chokes) block noise from propagating along the bus. Together they form a low-pass filter — passing clean DC while suppressing the RF interference generated by the alternator's rectifier.
Basic Electrical.source: Identify components that filter alternator noise

Basic Electrical.clue: Avionics, lighting, ignition systems, and the electric fuel pump are all loads powered by this system in a general aviation aircraft.
Basic Electrical.explanation: The DC electrical system powers essentially all electrically-dependent equipment in a GA aircraft: radios, navigation, lights, ignition, and the fuel pump. Loss of DC power is an immediate emergency requiring action.
Basic Electrical.source: State DC system purpose — power avionics, lights, ignition

Basic Electrical.clue: This component provides a low-resistance electrical path between avionics and aircraft structure for grounding, lightning protection, and EMI reduction.
Basic Electrical.explanation: Bonding jumpers ensure all metal components share a common electrical reference (ground), provide a low-impedance path to dissipate lightning strikes safely, and reduce EMI by preventing differential potentials between components.
Basic Electrical.source: Explain bonding jumper purpose

Basic Electrical.clue: Providing a low-resistance path between components and aircraft structure for current return and static charge dissipation are the two functions of this component.
Basic Electrical.explanation: Bonding jumpers enable DC current to return through the airframe (as the aircraft's common 'negative' conductor) and dissipate static charges before they build to levels that cause interference or ignition risk near fuel systems.
Basic Electrical.source: Explain bonding jumper purpose — current return and static discharge

Basic Electrical.clue: In a 28VDC aircraft electrical system, this protective device opens the circuit when current exceeds its rated value — protecting the wiring from overheating, not the connected equipment.
After pulling and resetting a tripped circuit breaker once, it trips again immediately. Per standard aircraft maintenance practice, the correct next action is this.
choices: Replace the circuit breaker with a higher-rated unit to prevent nuisance trips
Reset it one more time — breakers are allowed two resets before grounding the circuit
Install a jumper wire to bypass the breaker and continue the maintenance run
Leave the breaker pulled and troubleshoot the protected circuit for a short or overload

CNS Systems.clue: A GPS receiver must track at least this many satellites to compute 3D navigation with WAAS precision.
CNS Systems.explanation: Three satellites provide 2D position; a fourth satellite allows the receiver to solve for altitude as well, enabling 3D navigation. WAAS uses ground correction stations to improve accuracy for both axes.
CNS Systems.source: GPS requires 4 satellites for 3D nav with WAAS

CNS Systems.clue: These V-shaped or bent-dipole antennas tuned to 108–117.95 MHz receive signals from VOR ground stations.
CNS Systems.explanation: VOR antennas are V-shaped or bent-dipole designs, mounted on the vertical stabilizer or fuselage, tuned to the VOR frequency range of 108.0–117.95 MHz (shared with ILS localizer on 108.1–111.95 MHz odd decimals).
CNS Systems.source: VOR antenna type and frequency range

CNS Systems.clue: This navigation system provides slant-range distance to a ground station in nautical miles by measuring pulse round-trip time.
CNS Systems.explanation: DME (Distance Measuring Equipment) interrogates a ground station; the station replies; the receiver measures round-trip time and converts to nautical miles. The result is slant range — the diagonal distance, not horizontal ground distance.
CNS Systems.source: DME measures slant-range distance in nautical miles

CNS Systems.clue: Coded audio tones and blue, amber, or white cockpit lights at fixed distance points on an ILS approach are provided by this system.
CNS Systems.explanation: Marker beacons transmit at 75 MHz vertically. The outer marker (blue light, slow dashes) is ~4–7 NM from threshold; middle marker (amber, alternating dot-dash) is ~3,500 ft; inner marker (white, fast dots) is at the decision altitude point.
CNS Systems.source: Marker beacon provides tones and lights at ILS fixed points

CNS Systems.clue: On a VOR indicator, this control allows the pilot to select a desired radial for course tracking.
CNS Systems.explanation: The OBS (Omnibearing Selector) rotates the course card to select a desired VOR radial. When the CDI needle centers with the OBS set to a given radial, the aircraft is on that radial — TO or FROM the station as shown by the flag.
CNS Systems.source: OBS selects desired VOR radial

CNS Systems.clue: This FAA ground-based correction system improves GPS accuracy to enable precision approach guidance (LPV) in the United States.
CNS Systems.explanation: WAAS (Wide Area Augmentation System) uses a network of ground reference stations to measure GPS errors and broadcast corrections via geostationary satellites. This improves accuracy to ~1m, enabling LPV (Localizer Performance with Vertical guidance) approaches.
CNS Systems.source: WAAS is FAA ground-based correction for GPS precision approaches

CNS Systems.clue: These airborne systems detect electromagnetic discharges from storm cells and display bearing and relative intensity on a cockpit display for weather avoidance.
CNS Systems.explanation: Lightning detection systems (e.g., Stormscope, Strike Finder) detect the electromagnetic signature of lightning discharges and map storm cell bearing and activity level on a cockpit display, enabling crews to identify and avoid convective weather.
CNS Systems.source: Lightning detection systems detect and display storm bearing and intensity

CNS Systems.clue: Compared to VOR, this navigation system is less accurate, more susceptible to electrical interference, and only provides bearing TO the station without course deviation data.
CNS Systems.explanation: ADF/NDB navigation has two key limitations vs. VOR: (1) greater susceptibility to interference (especially thunderstorms, which cause the ADF needle to deflect toward lightning), and (2) it only gives a bearing pointer TO the station — no course deviation bar for precise tracking.
CNS Systems.source: Compare ADF/NDB to VOR — NDB less accurate, more interference

CNS Systems.clue: Before returning a VOR system to service, a technician uses this type of test equipment to verify receiver accuracy on multiple radials on the ground.
CNS Systems.explanation: A VOR ramp tester generates a simulated VOR signal that the aircraft receiver processes. The tech selects various radials on the tester and verifies the CDI accurately reflects each selected radial before approving the system for return to service.
CNS Systems.source: VOR ground test with ramp tester procedure

CNS Systems.clue: This precision approach system combines a localizer for horizontal guidance and a glideslope for vertical guidance during an instrument approach to landing.
CNS Systems.explanation: ILS is a precision approach system providing two axes of guidance: the localizer (lateral — aligns aircraft with runway centerline) and glideslope (vertical — provides the standard 3° descent path to the touchdown zone).
CNS Systems.source: ILS provides both vertical and horizontal approach guidance

CNS Systems.clue: VOR ground stations transmit signals that allow pilots to determine this type of information for course tracking along specific radials.
choices: Distance to the station in nautical miles
Magnetic bearing to and from the station
CNS Systems.explanation: VOR provides magnetic bearing information — specifically, radials that are magnetic bearings FROM the station. A pilot can use VOR to track a specific course to or from a station by selecting the desired radial with the OBS.
CNS Systems.source: VOR provides magnetic bearing to/from station via radials

CNS Systems.clue: For best VHF communication coverage, antennas should be mounted here, away from other antennas and metal structures that cause signal shadowing.
choices: Inside the avionics bay for protection from weather
On the wingtip to maximize separation from the fuselage
On the horizontal stabilizer for tail-away transmission
Low on the belly or on top of the fuselage, clear of obstructions
CNS Systems.explanation: VHF antennas mounted on the belly provide line-of-sight to ground stations; top-mounted antennas communicate with aircraft overhead. Both locations must be away from metal structures that would shadow (block) portions of the radiation pattern.
CNS Systems.source: Optimal VHF antenna mounting location

CNS Systems.clue: This VHF radio control sets the minimum signal threshold, muting background noise when no transmission is present.
CNS Systems.explanation: The squelch control sets the carrier or signal threshold that must be exceeded before audio is passed to the headset. When no one is transmitting, weak RF noise stays below the threshold and the headset remains quiet — no continuous static.
CNS Systems.source: Squelch mutes noise below signal threshold

CNS Systems.clue: Loose corroded connectors, damaged coaxial cable, and failing microphone jacks are the most common causes of this aircraft comm system symptom.
choices: Total loss of both transmit and receive capability
CNS Systems.explanation: Intermittent or noisy audio points to physical connection problems. Corroded connectors and damaged coax create resistance that introduces noise. A failing mic jack causes noise that varies with microphone movement — all three are physical/mechanical rather than electronic failures.
CNS Systems.source: Diagnose intermittent/noisy comm audio — connector, coax, mic jack

CNS Systems.clue: Physical condition, secure mounting, connector tightness, coaxial cable integrity, and absence of corrosion are all checked during this routine task.
CNS Systems.explanation: VHF antenna inspections cover all five items: physical element condition (no cracks), mounting security (no play), coax connector torque, cable inspection for damage, and corrosion check at the base and connector — all of which affect antenna performance and longevity.
CNS Systems.source: VHF antenna inspection checklist

CNS Systems.clue: When working on or near a VHF antenna or any RF-transmitting system on an aircraft, this safety measure is required.
choices: Wear rubber gloves only — RF is an electrical hazard
Ground the antenna with a static wick
Keep transmitter in standby mode at reduced power
CNS Systems.explanation: RF radiation from aircraft transmitters can cause thermal injury to body tissues at close range (especially eyes). The system must be completely OFF — not in standby — during any work on or near the antenna or transmission line.
CNS Systems.source: RF radiation safety — system off during antenna work

CNS Systems.clue: General aviation VHF communications use this frequency range, beginning at 118.0 MHz.
CNS Systems.explanation: Aviation VHF comm is 118.0–136.975 MHz. The range 108.0–117.95 MHz is shared by VOR and ILS (nav frequencies). 329–335 MHz is glideslope. 960–1215 MHz is DME and transponder.
CNS Systems.source: VHF comm frequency range 118.0–136.975 MHz

CNS Systems.clue: When replacing coaxial cable for a VHF antenna, correct impedance, proper connector installation and torque, and verifying this measurement are all required.
CNS Systems.explanation: After coax replacement, SWR (Standing Wave Ratio, also called VSWR) must be measured to verify the antenna-cable system is properly matched. An SWR above 1.5:1 indicates a mismatch — caused by wrong impedance cable, damaged connector, or antenna fault.
CNS Systems.source: Coaxial cable replacement — impedance, connectors, SWR

CNS Systems.clue: When a pilot hears a constant tone but cannot hear ATC responses, this failure — keeping the transmitter continuously keyed — is the most likely diagnosis.
choices: Stuck microphone PTT keeping the transmitter keyed
Audio panel failure routing TX audio to headset
CNS Systems.explanation: A stuck PTT keeps the transmitter on continuously, which blocks the receiver on the same frequency (you can't transmit and receive simultaneously on one radio). The constant sidetone in the headset is the transmitter sending back the mic signal. Other aircraft on the frequency also cannot transmit.
CNS Systems.source: Diagnose stuck mic — constant tone blocks reception

CNS Systems.clue: This radio frequency band is used for long-range aviation communications over oceans and remote areas where VHF line-of-sight coverage doesn't reach.
CNS Systems.explanation: HF uses ionospheric skip to propagate signals thousands of miles — far beyond VHF's line-of-sight range limit. Oceanic tracks, polar routes, and remote continental operations use HF (or satellite communications) because ATC VHF coverage doesn't exist over open oceans.
CNS Systems.source: HF used for long-range oceanic communications

CNS Systems.clue: This 4-digit code set by the pilot allows ATC radar to identify a specific aircraft and distinguish it from other traffic on the display.
CNS Systems.explanation: The squawk code (Mode A) is a 4-digit octal identifier set on the transponder at ATC request. When the radar interrogates, the transponder replies with this code, allowing controllers to label and track the specific aircraft's radar return among many.
CNS Systems.source: Squawk code identifies aircraft to ATC radar

CNS Systems.clue: This airborne collision avoidance system monitors nearby transponder-equipped aircraft and issues resolution advisories to prevent mid-air collisions.
CNS Systems.explanation: TCAS actively interrogates nearby transponders, computes collision risk, and issues TAs (awareness) and RAs (climb/descend instructions) to the crew. TCAS RA instructions override ATC instructions — crews must follow TCAS RAs immediately.
CNS Systems.source: TCAS monitors traffic and provides collision avoidance RAs

CNS Systems.clue: Replying to ATC secondary surveillance radar interrogations to allow positive aircraft identification and tracking is the primary purpose of this cockpit device.
CNS Systems.explanation: The transponder responds to ATC SSR interrogations at 1030 MHz with replies at 1090 MHz containing squawk code and altitude. This active response allows ATC to positively identify each aircraft rather than relying on passive primary radar returns alone.
CNS Systems.source: Primary transponder purpose — reply to ATC SSR interrogations

CNS Systems.clue: When installing a transponder antenna, verifying impedance match, using the correct coaxial cable, and torquing connectors to spec are all part of this procedure.
CNS Systems.explanation: Transponder antenna installation at 1030/1090 MHz requires: 50Ω impedance match (antenna and cable), correct low-loss coax for ~1 GHz operation, and properly installed/torqued connectors to ensure RF integrity and prevent water ingress.
CNS Systems.source: Transponder antenna installation — impedance, coax, torque

CNS Systems.clue: This unique 24-bit identifier assigned to a Mode S transponder allows ATC systems to selectively interrogate a specific aircraft rather than broadcast to all transponders.
CNS Systems.explanation: The 24-bit ICAO address is unique to each aircraft globally — assigned by the country of registration. Mode S ground stations use it to address specific aircraft individually (selective interrogation), reducing frequency congestion compared to Mode A/C all-call interrogations.
CNS Systems.source: Mode S 24-bit address enables selective interrogation

CNS Systems.clue: GPS position, altitude, velocity, identification, and state data are all included in the continuous broadcast of this avionics system.
CNS Systems.explanation: ADS-B Out broadcasts all of this state data automatically at approximately 1 Hz on 1090 MHz (Mode S Extended Squitter) or 978 MHz (UAT). It doesn't wait to be interrogated — it broadcasts continuously to both ATC ground stations and other ADS-B In equipped aircraft.
CNS Systems.source: ADS-B Out broadcasts position, altitude, velocity, ID

CNS Systems.clue: In addition to the Mode A squawk code, this transponder mode automatically transmits pressure altitude encoded by an encoding altimeter.
CNS Systems.explanation: Mode C adds automatic altitude reporting to the Mode A squawk code reply. The encoding altimeter (or ADC) provides pressure altitude in Gillham code; the transponder includes this in its reply. ATC sees both identity and altitude, enabling 3D tracking.
CNS Systems.source: Mode C transmits squawk plus pressure altitude

CNS Systems.clue: Transponder antennas are typically low-profile blade or stub antennas tuned to operate at these two frequencies.
CNS Systems.explanation: ATC interrogates at 1030 MHz; transponders reply at 1090 MHz. The antenna must cover both frequencies. Low-profile blades or stubs are used to minimize aerodynamic drag while maintaining adequate ground plane for the omnidirectional radiation pattern needed.
CNS Systems.source: Transponder antenna type and frequency range

CNS Systems.clue: This transponder mode simultaneously provides both aircraft identification (squawk code) and pressure altitude reporting in a single reply to ATC radar.
CNS Systems.explanation: Mode C combines Mode A (squawk code) with pressure altitude reporting. Mode A alone gives identity; Mode C gives identity + altitude. Mode S adds data link and unique 24-bit addressing. Mode C is the standard requirement for most controlled US airspace.
CNS Systems.source: Mode C provides both squawk code and altitude simultaneously

CNS Systems.clue: When integrating an audio panel with other avionics, this factor between audio panel output and connected equipment is the most important for achieving clear audio.
choices: Physical separation from the radio stack
Total cable length to each avionics unit
CNS Systems.explanation: Impedance matching is the primary factor for audio quality in avionics integration. Mismatched impedances cause signal reflections, level loss, and distortion. All audio interfaces in the chain must have compatible impedances for clean signal transfer.
CNS Systems.source: Impedance matching is most important factor for clear audio

CNS Systems.clue: Routing and selecting communication and navigation audio from multiple radios to pilot and copilot headsets and cockpit speakers is the primary purpose of this panel.
choices: The communication and navigation management unit
CNS Systems.explanation: The audio panel is the audio routing hub: it takes inputs from all communication and navigation receivers and allows each crewmember to select what they want to monitor. It also routes PTT signals to the selected COM radio for transmission.
CNS Systems.source: Audio panel routes comm/nav audio to pilot and copilot

CNS Systems.clue: A single centralized interface enabling crews to select, monitor, and transmit on multiple communication and navigation radios describes the primary purpose of this cockpit panel.
CNS Systems.explanation: The audio control panel centralizes all audio management: transmit selection (which COM to send on), receive selection (which COM/NAV/ADF to monitor), intercom control, and speaker selection — all from one panel accessible to both crew positions.
CNS Systems.source: Audio control panel enables crew to select and transmit on multiple radios

CNS Systems.clue: WAAS corrects GPS errors caused by ionospheric delays, satellite clock drift, and orbital position inaccuracies — enabling this type of precision approach that replaces traditional ILS at many airports.
choices: LPV approach with vertical guidance to 200-foot minimums
CAT III autoland approach with zero-visibility minimums

CNS Systems.clue: Before a GPS receiver can compute a navigation solution, it must determine each satellite's precise orbital position using this data, which is broadcast by each satellite and updated approximately every two hours.
A GPS-equipped aircraft is cleared for an LPV approach, but the WAAS annunciation shows "LNAV only" during the approach. This indicates the system has lost this critical capability.
choices: DME distance measurement to the runway threshold
Radar altimeter input for decision height verification
Vertical guidance from WAAS geostationary satellite corrections

CNS Systems.clue: This autonomous integrity monitoring function within the GPS receiver predicts whether satellite geometry will be adequate to support the planned approach — and must be checked during preflight for RNAV (GPS) approaches without WAAS.
Unlike VOR which provides bearing, DME measures this — calculated by timing the round-trip of paired radio pulses between the aircraft and a ground station.
An ADF needle points directly at a ground station transmitting on this frequency band, but the system is susceptible to shoreline refraction, night effect, and electrical storm interference.
This airborne weather detection system transmits its own X-band radar energy forward of the aircraft and paints precipitation returns on a cockpit display — but cannot detect clear air turbulence.
A DME interrogator sends pulse pairs on one frequency and listens for replies on a different frequency — this frequency offset between transmit and receive prevents this self-interference problem.
choices: The receiver triggering on its own transmitted pulses

CNS Systems.clue: When a newly installed audio panel produces a loud 400 Hz hum in all headsets, the most likely cause is this wiring fault in the audio ground reference.
choices: A ground loop between the audio panel chassis and the airframe ground bus
A reversed hot and neutral on the aircraft's AC essential bus
Excessive cable length exceeding the audio panel's rated output impedance
An incorrect squelch threshold setting on the installed comm radio

CNS Systems.clue: During an audio panel installation, this measurement between the panel's audio output and the headset jack must match the specified value to prevent distortion or excessively low volume.
choices: DC voltage bias on the microphone input
RF noise floor measured at the antenna port
Impedance match between audio output and the connected load
Current draw from the audio amplifier circuit breaker

CNS Systems.clue: In a dual-comm installation, the pilot's headset receives audio from Comm 1 but not Comm 2, even though Comm 2 transmits normally. The audio panel is set to receive both. The most likely fault is here.
choices: Comm 2's squelch circuit has failed open, blocking all received audio
The Comm 2 antenna has an open coaxial shield causing receive failure
The audio panel's Comm 2 volume potentiometer is set to minimum
The audio panel's Comm 2 receive wire or connection at the panel's input pin

Flight Instruments.clue: In a glass-cockpit aircraft, this computer processes pitot-static pressure data to supply airspeed and altitude information to the Primary Flight Display.
Flight Instruments.explanation: The ADC interfaces directly with the pitot-static system and converts pressure measurements into digital airspeed and altitude data for the PFD.
Flight Instruments.source: ADC as source of airspeed and altitude for PFD

Flight Instruments.clue: These solid-state devices, used in modern EFIS attitude sensing, measure angular rate and linear acceleration without any rotating or moving mechanical components.
Flight Instruments.explanation: EFIS systems use MEMS (Micro-Electro-Mechanical Systems) sensors — solid-state gyroscopes and accelerometers — to determine attitude and motion without mechanical moving parts.
Flight Instruments.source: EFIS uses MEMS gyroscopes and accelerometers for attitude data

Flight Instruments.clue: These six traditional round-dial instruments are consolidated onto a single Primary Flight Display in a glass-cockpit aircraft.
choices: ASI, ADI, altimeter, DME, DI, turn coordinator
ASI, ADI, altimeter, VSI, DI, ADF
ASI, ADI, altimeter, VSI, DI, turn coordinator
ASI, ADI, altimeter, VOR, DI, HSI
Flight Instruments.explanation: The PFD consolidates the ASI (airspeed), ADI (attitude), altimeter, VSI (vertical speed), DI (heading/directional gyro), and turn coordinator into one integrated display.
Flight Instruments.source: Traditional instruments replaced by modern PFD

Flight Instruments.clue: Before flight, this value in inches of mercury must be entered in the altimeter's Kollsman window so the instrument correctly reads altitude above sea level.
choices: Ram air pressure from the pitot tube
Flight Instruments.explanation: The local altimeter setting (QNH) is dialed into the Kollsman window to reference the altimeter to local atmospheric pressure, giving an accurate MSL altitude reading.
Flight Instruments.source: Altimeter must be set to local barometric pressure before flight

Flight Instruments.clue: In a vacuum-driven attitude indicator, air is drawn through this component by the vacuum system and directed at rotor buckets to spin the gyro.
Flight Instruments.explanation: The vacuum system draws filtered air through a nozzle aimed at buckets machined around the rotor rim; the jet of air impinges on the buckets to spin the gyro.
Flight Instruments.source: Vacuum system spins gyro rotor via air through nozzle

Flight Instruments.clue: When a pitot tube becomes completely blocked, this flight instrument is primarily affected because it needs ram air pressure to calculate airspeed.
Flight Instruments.explanation: The ASI uses the differential between ram (pitot) pressure and static pressure. A blocked pitot tube eliminates the ram pressure input, making the ASI unable to measure airspeed.
Flight Instruments.source: Blocked pitot tube primarily affects airspeed indicator

Flight Instruments.clue: Unlike the turn-and-bank indicator, this instrument senses both roll rate and yaw rate because its gyro is canted at approximately 30 degrees.
Flight Instruments.explanation: The turn coordinator's gyro is tilted at ~30° so it responds to both yaw and roll. The turn-and-bank indicator has a vertically mounted gyro that only senses yaw.
Flight Instruments.source: Turn coordinator senses roll rate and yaw rate; turn-and-bank senses only yaw rate

Flight Instruments.clue: Among primary flight instruments, this directional reference instrument requires no gyro or power source because it aligns directly to Earth's magnetic field.
Flight Instruments.explanation: The magnetic compass uses Earth's magnetic field — not a gyro. It works without electrical or vacuum power, making it the ultimate backup directional reference.
Flight Instruments.source: Magnetic compass is not gyroscopically driven

Flight Instruments.clue: During descent, a blocked pitot tube causes the ASI to read falsely high because static pressure increases while this pressure remains trapped and constant.
Flight Instruments.explanation: In descent, static port pressure rises. The trapped ram pressure stays constant. The ASI sees an increasing pitot-minus-static differential and registers a falsely high airspeed.
Flight Instruments.source: Blocked pitot during descent causes falsely high ASI reading

Flight Instruments.clue: This pitot-static instrument displays the rate of change of barometric altitude in feet per minute by sensing how quickly static pressure is changing.
Flight Instruments.explanation: The VSI uses a differential between a direct static port connection and a calibrated metering orifice to measure how fast static pressure is changing, giving a climb/descent rate in fpm.
Flight Instruments.source: VSI displays rate of change of barometric altitude

Flight Instruments.clue: A blocked static port causes these two instruments to become unusable: one freezes at the altitude where the blockage occurred, and the other reads zero continuously.
Flight Instruments.explanation: Both the altimeter and VSI rely on static pressure changes. A blocked static port traps pressure — the altimeter freezes at the blocked altitude, and the VSI reads zero (no pressure change detected).
Flight Instruments.source: Blocked static port freezes altimeter and VSI at zero

Flight Instruments.clue: These two flight instruments are commonly powered by the aircraft vacuum system, which spins their gyro rotors via air jets.
Flight Instruments.explanation: The AI and DG (heading indicator) are conventionally vacuum-driven. The ASI, altimeter, and VSI are pitot-static; the TC is typically electrically driven.
Flight Instruments.source: Attitude indicator and DG are vacuum-powered

Flight Instruments.clue: During a climb with a blocked pitot tube, static pressure decreases while this pressure stays constant, causing the ASI to show falsely increasing airspeed.
Flight Instruments.explanation: During climb, atmospheric pressure falls. Blocked pitot traps ram pressure constant. ASI measures pitot minus static — as static decreases, the differential grows, reading falsely high airspeed.
Flight Instruments.source: Blocked pitot during climb causes falsely increasing ASI reading

Flight Instruments.clue: The AHRS in a modern EFIS determines attitude, heading, and angular rates using these three types of solid-state sensors instead of spinning mechanical gyros.
choices: GPS receivers, barometric sensors, and radar altimeters
Pitot probes, static ports, and angle-of-attack vanes
MEMS chips, ADC units, and flux gate compasses only
Flight Instruments.explanation: AHRS systems combine rate gyros (angular rate), accelerometers (linear motion/gravity), and magnetometers (magnetic heading) in solid-state form — no mechanical spinning gyros needed.
Flight Instruments.source: AHRS uses rate gyros, accelerometers, magnetometers — no mechanical gyros

Flight Instruments.clue: An airspeed indicator that consistently reads lower than actual aircraft speed most likely has this type of fault in the pitot pressure system.
choices: Partial pitot tube blockage or leak in pitot plumbing
Flight Instruments.explanation: A consistent low reading means less ram pressure is reaching the ASI than expected. A partial blockage (reduced flow) or a leak (pressure escaping) in the pitot line both reduce effective ram pressure.
Flight Instruments.source: Consistently low ASI reading indicates partial pitot blockage or leak

Flight Instruments.clue: This value — 29.92 inches of mercury or 1013.25 millibars — represents the standard atmospheric pressure at sea level used as a reference in aviation.
choices: The minimum safe pressure for pressurized cabins
The altimeter correction factor for high-altitude flight
The maximum pitot static test pressure limit
Flight Instruments.explanation: 29.92 inHg (1013.25 mb/hPa) is the International Standard Atmosphere sea-level pressure. Altimeters set to this value read pressure altitude rather than true altitude.
Flight Instruments.source: Standard atmospheric pressure at sea level is 29.92 inHg

Flight Instruments.clue: This forward-facing probe senses and delivers ram air pressure — the key input to the airspeed indicator and air data computer.
Flight Instruments.explanation: The pitot tube collects ram (impact) air pressure and delivers it to the ASI and ADC. The pressure increases with airspeed, enabling airspeed computation.
Flight Instruments.source: Pitot tube senses and delivers ram air pressure to airspeed indicator

Flight Instruments.clue: The altimeter measures barometric altitude by sensing changes in this pressure through an aneroid capsule and converting expansion to a feet or meters reading.
choices: Total pressure from both pitot and static
