Flight Instruments.explanation: The altimeter uses only static pressure — sensed through the static port — feeding an aneroid capsule that expands as static pressure decreases with altitude increase.
Flight Instruments.source: Altimeter measures barometric altitude via static pressure changes

Flight Instruments.clue: Flush-mounted static ports supply ambient atmospheric pressure to these three flight instruments: the altimeter, VSI, and one more.
Flight Instruments.explanation: The static pressure system feeds three instruments: the altimeter (altitude reference), the VSI (rate of pressure change), and the airspeed indicator (static side of differential pressure).
Flight Instruments.source: Static port supplies altimeter, VSI, and ASI

Flight Instruments.clue: In the standard 'Basic T' instrument layout, the attitude indicator occupies this position, with the airspeed indicator to its left and the altimeter to its right.
Flight Instruments.explanation: The Basic T puts the AI at upper-center — the reference instrument for all cross-checks — with ASI upper-left, ALT upper-right, and DI/HI lower-center.
Flight Instruments.source: Standard T arrangement of flight instruments

Flight Instruments.clue: This gyroscopic property allows the attitude indicator's rotor to maintain a fixed orientation in space, resisting aircraft pitch and roll movements to provide a stable reference.
Flight Instruments.explanation: Rigidity in space (also called gyroscopic inertia) causes a spinning rotor to resist any force changing its axis orientation. This resistance provides the stable reference needed for the attitude indicator.
Flight Instruments.source: Gyroscopic rigidity in space allows stable attitude reference

Flight Instruments.clue: A VSI consistently shows a rate of descent in level flight because this fault allows static pressure to equalize faster than the instrument's calibrated metering orifice.
choices: A leak in the static system
Flight Instruments.explanation: A static system leak provides a path for pressure to equalize faster than the VSI's calibrated orifice. The VSI senses the higher pressure influx as an apparent descent, even in level flight.
Flight Instruments.source: VSI showing descent in level flight indicates static system leak

Flight Instruments.clue: These three flight instruments all rely on an accurate static port reading: the altimeter, the VSI, and this third instrument.
Flight Instruments.explanation: The ASI uses static pressure as the reference side of its differential measurement. Along with the altimeter and VSI, all three require a clean, accurate static port connection.
Flight Instruments.source: Altimeter, VSI, and ASI rely on static port

Digital Databus.clue: This avionics data bus standard uses two wires in a unidirectional configuration, allowing one transmitter to send data to multiple LRU receivers.
Digital Databus.explanation: ARINC 429 is the standard unidirectional, shielded twisted-pair serial bus used throughout general and commercial aviation for LRU-to-LRU data exchange.
Digital Databus.source: ARINC 429 is a unidirectional two-wire serial bus for avionics LRU communication

Digital Databus.clue: The two standard ARINC 429 data rates are this low-speed range and a high-speed rate of 100 kbps.
Digital Databus.explanation: ARINC 429 defines low-speed operation at 12.5–14.5 kbps and high-speed operation at 100 kbps. High speed is used for critical flight data; low speed for less time-critical data.
Digital Databus.source: ARINC 429 speeds: 12.5 kbps low speed, 100 kbps high speed

Digital Databus.clue: Before replacing a suspected faulty ARINC 429 LRU due to slow data updates, a technician should first determine whether this condition or an infrequent source is causing the latency.
Digital Databus.explanation: High bus loading means more labels are being transmitted per cycle, extending the time between updates for any single label. This is a configuration issue, not a hardware failure.
Digital Databus.source: High latency on ARINC 429 indicates high bus loading or infrequent data source

Digital Databus.clue: A digital databus in avionics is designed to provide this primary benefit: enabling reliable, standardized data transfer between multiple avionics units over a shared medium.
choices: Increased electrical power distribution to avionics
Reliable standardized data transfer between multiple LRUs
Digital Databus.explanation: The fundamental purpose of a databus is standardized, reliable communication between LRUs — replacing analog point-to-point wiring with a shared digital medium.
Digital Databus.source: Digital databus purpose: standardized reliable data transfer between LRUs

Digital Databus.clue: In ARINC 429, a single transmitted data unit contains exactly this many bits, including the 8-bit label and data fields.
Digital Databus.explanation: ARINC 429 uses a fixed 32-bit word structure for every transmitted data word, regardless of the type of data being sent.
Digital Databus.source: ARINC 429 uses 32-bit word structure

Digital Databus.clue: When troubleshooting intermittent ARINC 429 communication loss, this should be the first step before suspecting a failed LRU.
choices: Replace the transmitting LRU with a known-good unit
Check wiring continuity and connector integrity at both ends
Reprogram the receiving LRU's ARINC label configuration
Install a bus signal repeater to boost signal strength
Digital Databus.explanation: Intermittent faults are most commonly caused by loose connectors or wiring issues. Checking continuity and connector integrity at both ends is the correct first step per systematic troubleshooting.
Digital Databus.source: Intermittent ARINC 429 loss: first check wiring and connector integrity

Digital Databus.clue: Before replacing a non-responding MIL-STD-1553 remote terminal, a technician should first check these three potential causes: wiring, bus controller configuration, and this.
Digital Databus.explanation: A wrong remote terminal (RT) address means the device will never receive its command — it will appear to not respond even though it's otherwise functional.
Digital Databus.source: MIL-STD-1553 device not responding: check controller config, RT address, wiring

Digital Databus.clue: This key advantage makes digital data buses more reliable than analog signals in the electrically noisy avionics environment.
choices: Greater noise immunity and interference resistance
Digital Databus.explanation: Digital signals represent data as discrete states (0/1). Small amounts of electrical noise don't change the state unless they exceed the noise margin, providing far greater reliability than analog in noisy environments.
Digital Databus.source: Digital buses provide greater noise immunity than analog signals

Digital Databus.clue: The ARINC 429 high-speed bus transmits data at this maximum rate, used for time-critical avionics parameters.
Digital Databus.explanation: ARINC 429 high speed is 100 kbps. This is used for time-critical data like attitude, airspeed, and navigation that must be updated rapidly.
Digital Databus.source: ARINC 429 high-speed rate is 100 kbps

Digital Databus.clue: Before replacing any LRU on an ARINC 429 bus showing data errors, a technician should first measure these two parameters at the bus connection.
choices: Bit error rate and frame count
Bus voltage levels and termination resistance
Temperature and humidity at the avionics bay
Digital Databus.explanation: ARINC 429 signal quality depends on proper differential voltage levels (+10V/0V/-10V) and correct termination resistance (75–85 Ω). Both affect the ability of receivers to decode data accurately.
Digital Databus.source: ARINC 429 data errors: first verify bus voltage and termination resistance

Digital Databus.clue: This describes ARINC 429's physical topology: a single transmitter on shielded twisted-pair wiring that sends data to one or more receivers that cannot transmit back.
choices: Unidirectional broadcast on shielded twisted pair
Digital Databus.explanation: ARINC 429 is a unidirectional broadcast bus — one transmitter, one or more receivers, on shielded twisted pair. Communication is strictly one-way on any given bus pair.
Digital Databus.source: ARINC 429 is unidirectional broadcast on shielded twisted pair

Digital Databus.clue: The main engineering reason to use a digital databus instead of individual analog connections in an avionics system is this benefit.
choices: Higher signal voltage enabling longer cable runs
Lower cost per wire compared to analog coaxial cable
Elimination of the need for connector maintenance
Reduced wiring complexity and improved system integration between LRUs
Digital Databus.explanation: Digital buses replace many point-to-point analog connections with a single shared bus, dramatically reducing wire count, connector count, and improving overall system integration.
Digital Databus.source: Digital buses reduce wiring complexity and improve system integration

Digital Databus.clue: In ARINC 429 encoding, a binary '1' bit is represented by this voltage differential between the A and B lines.
Digital Databus.explanation: ARINC 429 tri-state encoding: +10V differential = binary '1', 0V = NULL, -10V = binary '0'. The A line is positive and B is negative for a logic '1'.
Digital Databus.source: ARINC 429 binary '1' is approximately +10V differential between A and B

Digital Databus.clue: This serial interface standard uses single-ended voltage levels for asynchronous point-to-point communication and is typically limited to cable runs of about 15 meters.
Digital Databus.explanation: RS-232 is a point-to-point, single-ended, asynchronous interface with a ~15 meter practical cable limit due to capacitance and noise susceptibility.
Digital Databus.source: RS-232: point-to-point asynchronous, single-ended, ≤15m

Digital Databus.clue: RS-232 transmits data using this signal type, where a single conductor referenced to ground represents the data, unlike differential standards that use two wires per signal.
Digital Databus.explanation: RS-232 is single-ended — one signal conductor references to common ground. This contrasts with differential standards like RS-485 and ARINC 429 that use two wires per signal.
Digital Databus.source: RS-232 is asynchronous single-ended serial communication

Digital Databus.clue: RS-485 achieves noise immunity and long-distance multi-drop communication by using this wiring configuration where data is encoded as the voltage difference between two conductors.
choices: Coaxial cable with center conductor and shield
Digital Databus.explanation: RS-485 uses differential twisted-pair: noise affects both wires equally and cancels out in the differential measurement, enabling long distances (up to 1,200m) and multi-drop topologies.
Digital Databus.source: RS-485 uses differential twisted-pair for noise-resistant multi-drop communication

Digital Databus.clue: For a correct RS-485 installation, the cable type is shielded twisted-pair, and the bus must be terminated at both ends using this method.
choices: The characteristic impedance (typically 120 Ω) at both ends
A 1 MΩ pull-up resistor at one end only
A 75 Ω coaxial terminator at the transmitter only
Digital Databus.explanation: RS-485 buses must be terminated at both ends with the characteristic impedance (typically 120 Ω). Unterminated or single-end terminated buses cause reflections that corrupt data.
Digital Databus.source: RS-422/RS-485 wiring: shielded twisted pair, terminated at both ends

Digital Databus.clue: A MIL-STD-1553 bus stuck in non-idle state longer than expected is caused by either a rogue remote terminal or this unit failing to release the bus.
choices: A stub cable with incorrect impedance
The bus controller failing to release the bus
A termination resistor that has failed open
An RT that has lost its address strapping
Digital Databus.explanation: The two causes of stuck non-idle on MIL-STD-1553 are: (1) an RT transmitting without a valid command (rogue), and (2) the bus controller hanging and failing to release the bus to idle.
Digital Databus.source: MIL-STD-1553 stuck non-idle: RT transmitting out of turn or BC not releasing bus

Digital Databus.clue: Unlike ARINC 429's one-way broadcast topology, this military databus standard uses bidirectional command-response communication managed by a central bus controller.
Digital Databus.explanation: MIL-STD-1553 is bidirectional with a Bus Controller commanding Remote Terminals. ARINC 429 is strictly unidirectional with no bus controller required.
Digital Databus.source: ARINC 429 unidirectional broadcast vs MIL-STD-1553 command-response

Digital Databus.clue: Before performing a functional check of a digital autopilot, a technician should verify ARINC 429 bus termination and confirm that the autopilot is receiving this type of data on all input buses.
choices: Manufacturer test vectors from a data loader
Valid sensor data from all connected ARINC 429 sources
Digital Databus.explanation: A digital autopilot requires valid sensor inputs (attitude, airspeed, navigation) on its ARINC 429 buses. Improper termination or missing valid data will cause the autopilot to fault or produce a test failure unrelated to the autopilot itself.
Digital Databus.source: Verify ARINC 429 termination and valid sensor data before autopilot functional check

Digital Databus.clue: This self-diagnostic system, embedded in avionics LRUs, monitors internal unit health during operation and stores fault codes that maintenance personnel can retrieve.
Digital Databus.explanation: BITE is integrated into each LRU, continuously monitoring internal functions and recording fault codes. Technicians retrieve BITE data to diagnose failures without needing external test equipment for basic fault identification.
Digital Databus.source: BITE: self-diagnostic system in LRUs that stores fault codes

Digital Databus.clue: ARINC 429 and other avionics digital data buses use this cable type, which combines twisting to reduce magnetic interference with a conductive shield to block electromagnetic coupling.
Digital Databus.explanation: Shielded twisted-pair is the standard for avionics digital buses: the twist cancels differential noise, and the shield reduces capacitive coupling and EMI — both essential in aircraft electrical environments.
Digital Databus.source: Shielded twisted pair is used for digital data transfer to reduce EMI

Digital Databus.clue: When a flight control computer is suspected of sending corrupted attitude data via ARINC 429, the first step before replacing the computer is to use this tool to examine the actual bus data.
choices: A multimeter to check bus voltage polarity
A wiring diagram to trace the signal path
A bus analyzer to capture and verify the data content at the bus
A BITE interrogator to read the flight computer's fault log
Digital Databus.explanation: A bus analyzer captures actual ARINC 429 words as transmitted. This confirms whether the flight computer is actually sending bad data or if the bus/wiring is corrupting good data downstream.
Digital Databus.source: Suspected bus corruption: first isolate with bus analyzer, don't replace LRU

Digital Databus.clue: In a MIL-STD-1553 system, this unit initiates all data transfers by issuing command words to remote terminals in a scheduled sequence.
Digital Databus.explanation: The Bus Controller (BC) is the master of MIL-STD-1553. No RT transmits unless the BC issues a command — the BC schedules all data transfers by sending command words to each RT.
Digital Databus.source: MIL-STD-1553 bus controller schedules all transfers by issuing command words

Digital Databus.clue: This military databus standard operates at 1 Mbps with a Bus Controller issuing command words to Remote Terminals on a dual-redundant shielded twisted-pair cable.
Digital Databus.explanation: MIL-STD-1553 is the military command-response bus: BC commands RTs, bidirectional at 1 Mbps, on dual-redundant shielded twisted-pair (Bus A and Bus B).
Digital Databus.source: MIL-STD-1553: command-response bidirectional dual-redundant bus

Digital Databus.clue: The primary purpose of ARINC 429 in modern avionics is to provide this benefit, ensuring that LRUs from different manufacturers can exchange data reliably.
choices: Standardized digital data exchange between avionics LRUs
Encrypted secure communications for military applications
Real-time backup power to avionics computers
Digital Databus.explanation: ARINC 429 standardizes digital data exchange so that LRUs from different manufacturers use a common interface, enabling interoperability and system integration.
Digital Databus.source: ARINC 429 primary purpose: standardized digital data exchange between LRUs

Digital Databus.clue: All digital avionics data — from ARINC 429 words to BITE fault codes — is ultimately represented as combinations of these two digits.
Digital Databus.explanation: All digital data is binary at the fundamental level — sequences of 0s and 1s. Higher representations like hex are just shorthand for groups of binary digits.
Digital Databus.source: Binary (base-2) is the number system for digital avionics data

Digital Databus.clue: This digital databus standard is found in the majority of general aviation and commercial avionics installations, from glass cockpits to transport category aircraft.
Digital Databus.explanation: ARINC 429 is the dominant avionics databus standard in general aviation and commercial transport aircraft. Its prevalence makes it the standard all technicians must understand.
Digital Databus.source: ARINC 429 is a common digital databus standard in avionics

Digital Databus.clue: ARINC 429 uses this bus topology where a single LRU transmits on a two-wire bus and one or more LRUs receive the broadcast.
choices: Command-response with BC managing all RTs
Digital Databus.explanation: ARINC 429 topology: one transmitter per bus, one or more receivers. The transmitter continuously broadcasts; any connected receiver can listen.
Digital Databus.source: ARINC 429 uses single-transmitter to multiple-receivers topology

Digital Databus.clue: This fundamental unit of digital information can represent exactly two states — 0 or 1 — and is the basic building block of all digital avionics data.
Digital Databus.explanation: A bit is the smallest unit of digital data — a single 0 or 1. Bytes, words, and labels are all groups of bits. Everything in digital avionics reduces to bits at the fundamental level.
Digital Databus.source: Bit (binary digit) is the fundamental unit of digital systems

Digital Databus.clue: ARINC 429 is described as this type of data bus because digital data flows in one direction only — from the transmitting LRU to receiving LRUs.
Digital Databus.explanation: ARINC 429 is unidirectional: data flows from transmitter to receiver(s) only, on a serial bit stream. There is no return channel on the same bus.
Digital Databus.source: ARINC 429 is a unidirectional serial databus for avionics

Digital Databus.clue: Aeronautical Radio Incorporated defined this common digital databus standard that is used in general aviation and commercial avionics for LRU data exchange.
Digital Databus.explanation: ARINC 429, defined by Aeronautical Radio Incorporated, is the common industry standard for digital data exchange between avionics LRUs in GA and commercial aviation.
Digital Databus.source: ARINC 429 is a commonly used digital databus standard

Digital Databus.clue: Unlike Ethernet or MIL-STD-1553, an ARINC 429 data bus has only one transmitter per wire pair — meaning data flows in only one direction. This architecture is described as this.
An ARINC 429 word is exactly 32 bits long. Bits 9 and 10 carry the Sign/Status Matrix (SSM), which tells the receiving system this critical information about the data in the word.
choices: The transmitting LRU's part number and software version
Whether the data is valid, a test value, or a failure warning
The bus speed (high or low) at which the word was transmitted
The encryption key required to decode the BCD/BNR payload

Digital Databus.clue: Unlike ARINC 429's single-transmitter architecture, MIL-STD-1553 allows up to 31 remote terminals on a shared bus — all controlled by this single device that initiates every data transfer.
MIL-STD-1553 uses dual redundant buses (Bus A and Bus B) with transformer-coupled stubs. This redundancy design ensures continued operation when this occurs.
choices: The bus controller software encounters a runtime exception
More than 31 remote terminals are connected simultaneously
Data words exceed the 20-bit payload maximum
One bus suffers a physical fault such as an open or short

Digital Databus.clue: RS-232 serial communication between two avionics devices requires at minimum these three connections: transmit data (TXD), receive data (RXD), and this common reference.
When connecting an RS-232 port from a GPS receiver to an MFD, TXD from one device must connect to RXD on the other. If both TXD pins are connected together, this is the result.
choices: Data is received at half the configured baud rate
No data is received by either device
Both devices receive each other's data simultaneously
The higher-voltage device overrides the lower-voltage device

Digital Databus.clue: RS-232 signal levels swing between positive and negative voltages (typically ±5V to ±12V), which makes them incompatible with this modern logic standard used inside digital avionics — requiring a level converter chip at the interface.
choices: Current-loop signaling used by older teletype equipment
Fiber-optic signaling used by AFDX networks

Digital Databus.clue: When troubleshooting an ARINC 429 bus with no data output, verifying this physical measurement at the transmitter's output pins with an oscilloscope confirms whether the transmitting LRU is actually driving the bus.
choices: DC supply voltage at the LRU's power input connector
Ground continuity between the LRU chassis and airframe ground
The baud rate setting in the LRU configuration menu
A differential voltage swing (±5V high-speed or ±2.5V low-speed) on the twisted pair

Digital Databus.clue: A bus analyzer shows Label 310 (heading) arriving at the correct rate from the AHRS, but the PFD displays a red HDG flag. The label data shows SSM bits set to "No Computed Data." This tells the technician the fault is here.
choices: Inside the AHRS — it is transmitting but reporting that it cannot compute a valid heading
In the wiring between the AHRS and PFD — causing data corruption
In the PFD software — it is failing to parse a correctly formatted label
In the bus termination resistor — causing signal reflections that corrupt the SSM bits

Aircraft Wiring.clue: This wiring feature prevents mechanical tension on a wire from being transferred to connector pins or solder joints, reducing the risk of intermittent connections.
Aircraft Wiring.explanation: Strain relief clamps the wire near the connector so any pulling force is absorbed by the clamp rather than transmitted to the pins or solder joints — preventing intermittent faults.
Aircraft Wiring.source: Strain relief prevents tension transfer to connector pins or solder joints

Aircraft Wiring.clue: For aircraft wire termination, the preferred tool is this type of crimp tool because it cannot be released until the crimp cycle is fully completed, ensuring a consistent connection.
Aircraft Wiring.explanation: A calibrated ratcheting crimp tool locks at the correct crimp force and cannot be released prematurely, ensuring full crimp completion every time — the FAA-preferred method for aircraft wiring.
Aircraft Wiring.source: Properly sized crimped terminals with calibrated ratcheting tool are preferred

Aircraft Wiring.clue: The primary function of connectors in aircraft wiring is to provide reliable electrical junctions between harnesses and avionics units that can be disconnected for this purpose.
choices: Ease of installation and maintenance (removable junction)
Aircraft Wiring.explanation: Connectors allow LRUs to be removed and replaced without re-wiring the aircraft. This removability is the key function — reliable, repeatable electrical junctions for ease of maintenance.
Aircraft Wiring.source: Connector function: reliable removable electrical junction for maintenance

Aircraft Wiring.clue: The critical step in making a reliable solder joint is to heat this — not the solder — so the solder flows into the joint rather than balling up on a cold surface.
choices: The heat-shrink tubing adjacent to the joint
The solder wire only before touching the joint
Aircraft Wiring.explanation: Heating the work (wire + terminal) ensures solder wets into the joint properly. Applying solder directly to the iron's tip produces cold joints that look shiny but have poor mechanical and electrical integrity.
Aircraft Wiring.source: Solder joints: heat the work before applying solder

Aircraft Wiring.clue: This military-specification wire, using PTFE (Teflon) insulation, is the most common choice in modern GA aircraft for its light weight, high temperature rating, and resistance to chemicals and abrasion.
Aircraft Wiring.explanation: MIL-W-22759 PTFE (Teflon) wire is the dominant wire type in modern GA aircraft. Its combination of light weight, high temperature rating, chemical resistance, and durability makes it the standard.
Aircraft Wiring.source: PTFE/MIL-W-22759 is most common wire insulation in GA aircraft

Aircraft Wiring.clue: Before finalizing an avionics installation, this test involves gently flexing wires near connectors to detect intermittent connections that static continuity tests would not find.
Aircraft Wiring.explanation: The wiggle test simulates in-service wire movement. Intermittent connections from loose pins or marginal crimps will show up as momentary circuit breaks during the flex test.
Aircraft Wiring.source: Wiggle test detects intermittent connections not found in static testing

Aircraft Wiring.clue: This component on the rear of an aircraft connector clamps the wire bundle to provide strain relief, protect pin terminations from vibration, and (in sealed types) exclude moisture.
Aircraft Wiring.explanation: The backshell serves three functions: mechanical strain relief (clamps the bundle), protection of pin terminations (against vibration), and environmental sealing against moisture and contaminants.
Aircraft Wiring.source: Backshells provide mechanical strain relief and protect terminations

Aircraft Wiring.clue: When routing an aircraft wire bundle, it must be kept away from these four hazard types to prevent chafing and insulation damage: heat sources, moving parts, sharp edges, and these.
Aircraft Wiring.explanation: The four wire routing hazard categories are: heat sources, moving parts, sharp edges, and fuel/fluid lines. Fluid line leaks combined with damaged wire insulation can create fire or short circuit hazards.
Aircraft Wiring.source: Wire routing: keep away from heat, moving parts, sharp edges, fluid lines

Aircraft Wiring.clue: To select the correct wire gauge for an aircraft installation, a technician must consult the wire load analysis and manufacturer's ampacity tables, considering current load, run length, and this factor.
choices: Temperature environment of the installation area
Number of connectors in the circuit
Aircraft Wiring.explanation: Wire gauge selection depends on three factors: current load (ampacity), wire run length (voltage drop), and temperature environment (de-rating of ampacity in high-temp zones). All three must be considered.
Aircraft Wiring.source: Select wire gauge using wire load analysis and ampacity tables

Aircraft Wiring.clue: When wiring an aircraft lighting system to prevent EMI in adjacent avionics, power wires should be separated from signal wires, and these cable types should be used for sensitive signals.
Aircraft Wiring.explanation: The two key EMI reduction techniques are: (1) physical separation of power and signal wires, and (2) using twisted-pair or shielded cable for sensitive signals to reduce coupling from nearby power wiring.
Aircraft Wiring.source: EMI reduction: separate power/signal wires, use twisted pair or shielded cable

Aircraft Wiring.clue: When a wire bundle is too large for its designated routing path, the correct first action is to consult this document for an approved alternate routing solution.
choices: The aircraft logbook for previous maintenance entries
The shop supervisor to determine if the bundle can be forced in
The installation manual for an approved alternate route
The wire manufacturer's data sheet for a thinner equivalent
Aircraft Wiring.explanation: The installation manual is the authority for all routing decisions. An approved alternate route maintains airworthiness; forcing a bundle or trimming wires without approval compromises the certified design.
Aircraft Wiring.source: Wire bundle too large for routing path: consult manual for alternate route

Aircraft Wiring.clue: The standard shield termination practice for avionics wiring grounds the drain wire at one end only to prevent this type of interference caused by potential differences between two ground points.
Aircraft Wiring.explanation: Grounding a shield at both ends creates a ground loop — a closed conductor loop that acts as a transformer secondary. Any AC difference between the two ground points drives circulating current, creating EMI.
Aircraft Wiring.source: Shield termination: ground at one end only to prevent ground loops

Aircraft Wiring.clue: In the high-temperature zone adjacent to an aircraft engine, wire insulation must be rated for elevated temperatures; PTFE (MIL-W-22759/16) or this type of insulation is typically required.
Aircraft Wiring.explanation: Fiberglass-insulated wire handles temperatures up to 400°C+ and is used in the most extreme heat zones near engines. PTFE (MIL-W-22759) is used for moderate-high temperature zones.
Aircraft Wiring.source: High-temperature wire required near aircraft engines

Aircraft Wiring.clue: For installing contacts in aircraft multi-pin connectors, two specialized tools are required: a ratcheting crimp tool and these, which seat and remove contacts without damaging the connector insert.
choices: Needle-nose pliers and a pick set
Aircraft Wiring.explanation: Contact insertion tools push the crimp contact into the connector insert until it locks; extraction tools release the retention clip to remove a contact. Using pliers or picks risks damaging contacts and inserts.
Aircraft Wiring.source: Ratcheting crimp tool and contact insertion/extraction tools for connector work

Aircraft Wiring.clue: A properly crimped aircraft wire terminal creates this type of connection by mechanically deforming metal tightly around wire strands, excluding air and preventing oxidation.
choices: A soldered joint with rosin flux residue
A gas-tight, low-resistance electrical and mechanical connection
A press-fit thermal joint using heat-activated adhesive
Aircraft Wiring.explanation: The crimp process deforms the terminal barrel tightly around wire strands, creating gas-tight metal-to-metal contact. No air means no oxidation, producing a stable, low-resistance, permanently secure connection.
Aircraft Wiring.source: Wire crimper creates gas-tight low-resistance connection by mechanical deformation

Aircraft Wiring.clue: Three practices prohibited in aircraft wiring installations are: using automotive-grade connectors, leaving unterminated wire ends unprotected, and this.
choices: Using PTFE-insulated wire in ambient temperature areas
Using ratcheting crimp tools for contact installation
Installing heat-shrink tubing over connector backshells
Soldering wires to contacts designed for crimping
Aircraft Wiring.explanation: Soldering onto crimp contacts wicks solder up the wire, creating a brittle heat-affected zone at the solder line — a common point of wire fracture under vibration. Crimp contacts must be crimped, not soldered.
Aircraft Wiring.source: Prohibited wiring practices: soldering crimp contacts, automotive connectors, unprotected wire ends

Aircraft Wiring.clue: A shielded cable provides no EMI protection when the shield is floating (ungrounded) at both ends because induced interference currents have no path to flow here.
Aircraft Wiring.explanation: A grounded shield provides a low-impedance path to ground for induced currents. A floating shield cannot divert these currents — they have nowhere to go except into the signal conductors.
Aircraft Wiring.source: Shield floating at both ends provides no EMI protection

Aircraft Wiring.clue: In the AWG system, a 22 AWG wire is smaller in diameter than a 16 AWG wire because in this numbering system, higher numbers indicate smaller conductors.
choices: A 16 AWG wire is smaller because lower numbers indicate finer wire
A 22 AWG wire is smaller — higher AWG numbers mean smaller conductor cross-section
Both wires are the same size because AWG only specifies insulation thickness
A 22 AWG wire has a larger conductor but thinner insulation than 16 AWG
Aircraft Wiring.explanation: In AWG, higher number = smaller wire. 22 AWG < 16 AWG in diameter. This counterintuitive system is important to know when selecting wire for current-carrying capacity.
Aircraft Wiring.source: AWG: higher number = smaller wire

Aircraft Wiring.clue: Shielded wire in avionics systems prevents external EMI from coupling in AND prevents signal emissions from doing this.
choices: Creating voltage drops along the run
Aircraft Wiring.explanation: Shielded wire provides bi-directional EMI protection: it blocks external fields from reaching the signal conductor AND contains signal emissions that could interfere with adjacent avionics.
Aircraft Wiring.source: Shielded wire prevents EMI coupling into and emission from sensitive signal wires

Aircraft Wiring.clue: In avionics work, 60/40 tin-lead rosin-core solder is standard; this type of solder is strictly prohibited because its flux leaves a corrosive residue that attacks conductors.
Aircraft Wiring.explanation: Acid-core solder contains an acidic flux that remains on the joint after soldering, continuing to corrode copper conductors and contacts. It is absolutely prohibited in avionics work.
Aircraft Wiring.source: 60/40 rosin-core solder used; acid-core prohibited

Aircraft Wiring.clue: Automotive-grade wire connectors are unacceptable in aircraft avionics racks because they fail to meet this regulatory requirement for aviation maintenance materials.
choices: FAA-approved material standards required under Part 43
Aircraft Wiring.explanation: Part 43 requires that maintenance use materials and parts that meet or exceed FAA-approved standards. Automotive connectors are designed for automotive environments and do not meet aviation temperature, vibration, or altitude specifications.
Aircraft Wiring.source: Automotive connectors not acceptable in avionics — not FAA-approved materials

Aircraft Wiring.clue: When replacing a wire connector, the ratcheting crimp tool must be fitted with a die that matches both the contact type and this, to ensure a correct gas-tight termination.
Aircraft Wiring.explanation: The crimp die must match both the contact/terminal type AND the wire gauge. The die geometry sets the crimp depth — too shallow (under-crimp) leaves a loose connection; too deep (over-crimp) damages the contact.
Aircraft Wiring.source: Select correct crimping tool and die for connector and wire size

Aircraft Wiring.clue: When stripping PTFE-insulated wire, automatic stripper blade depth must be set precisely to avoid this defect that weakens the conductor and can cause eventual wire breakage.
choices: Insulation wicking up the stripped area
Stretching the insulation beyond the strip length
Aircraft Wiring.explanation: Nicking conductor strands weakens the wire at the strip point — a nicked conductor has reduced cross-section and will fail under vibration or bending stress at the nick.
Aircraft Wiring.source: Teflon wire stripping: verify blade depth to avoid nicking conductor

Aircraft Wiring.clue: The correct first step when fabricating an aircraft wire harness from a wiring diagram is to study the diagram completely — understanding wire runs, connector locations, and pin assignments — before doing this.
Aircraft Wiring.explanation: Studying the diagram first is essential — harness errors discovered after cutting wire waste materials and require re-work. Understanding the complete wire list before cutting is the first and most critical step.
Aircraft Wiring.source: Harness fabrication first step: study the diagram completely before cutting

Aircraft Wiring.clue: In aircraft wheel wells and engine bays where moisture and fluids are present, this type of wire splice is preferred to protect the connection from environmental contamination.
choices: Environmentally sealed splice (solder-sleeve or adhesive-lined heat-shrink)
Aircraft Wiring.explanation: Environmentally sensitive areas require splices that are sealed against moisture and fluids — solder-sleeve splices or adhesive-lined heat-shrink over crimp splices provide this protection.
Aircraft Wiring.source: Environmentally sealed splices preferred in exposed areas

Aircraft Wiring.clue: When selecting wire insulation for an aircraft installation, it must be matched to the environment's maximum temperature, potential chemical exposure, and this third factor.
choices: Abrasion resistance of the installation area
The wire's minimum bend radius requirement
Aircraft Wiring.explanation: The three environmental matching criteria for wire insulation are: temperature rating, chemical resistance, and abrasion resistance. All three must be appropriate for the installation location.
Aircraft Wiring.source: Wire insulation selection: match temperature, chemical, and abrasion ratings to environment

Aircraft Wiring.clue: An aircraft wire harness must be supported at regular intervals using approved clamps, kept clear of moving parts and heat, and routed to avoid this type of bend that can damage conductors.
choices: Sharp bends below the minimum bend radius
Aircraft Wiring.explanation: Sharp bends below the minimum bend radius damage insulation, kink conductors, and fracture strands over time. Approved clamps at correct intervals prevent movement and chafing.
Aircraft Wiring.source: Harness routing: supported at intervals, clear of moving parts and heat

Aircraft Wiring.clue: Three acceptable methods for securing aircraft wire harnesses are: approved lacing cord, cushioned clamps at specified intervals, and this method with edge protectors where needed.
Aircraft Wiring.explanation: Nylon tie wraps (with edge protection where the harness contacts structure) are an approved harness securing method along with lacing cord and cushioned AdEL-type clamps.
Aircraft Wiring.source: Harness securing methods: lacing cord, nylon tie wraps, cushioned clamps

Aircraft Wiring.clue: The extra wire coiled near a connector in a harness installation is called a service loop; its purpose is to allow re-termination or repair in the future without this costly fix.
choices: Running an entirely new wire from the equipment
Aircraft Wiring.explanation: Service loops provide wire for future re-terminations. Without a service loop, stripping and re-terminating a wire eventually uses up all the length, requiring a splice to add more wire.
Aircraft Wiring.source: Service loop provides extra wire for future re-termination or repair

Aircraft Wiring.clue: Wire bundles routed near moving flight controls must be installed with approved conduit or spiral wrap AND this must be provided to prevent restriction of control movement.
Aircraft Wiring.explanation: Slack is essential — a tight bundle near flight controls can be pinched or stretched when the control moves through its full range, risking wire damage or worse, restricting control movement.
Aircraft Wiring.source: Wire bundles near flight controls: conduit with slack, prevent contact

Aircraft Wiring.clue: When a wire bundle is found chafed against a metal edge, the first step is to remove power, then assess the full extent of damage, and then follow only approved procedures from this document.
choices: The aircraft weight and balance report
The applicable maintenance manual or FAA AC 43.13
The avionics shop's standard operating procedure
Aircraft Wiring.explanation: Approved repair procedures from the maintenance manual or AC 43.13 are the only acceptable methods for repairing chafed wiring. Unapproved repairs render the aircraft unairworthy.
Aircraft Wiring.source: Chafed wire bundle: remove power, assess damage, follow approved repair procedure

Aircraft Wiring.clue: A nicked coaxial cable center conductor requires complete cable replacement because the nick creates a local impedance change and compromises this property of the conductor.
Aircraft Wiring.explanation: A nick reduces the center conductor cross-section, compromising mechanical strength (vibration fatigue) and changing local impedance (causing signal reflections). Neither is repairable — the run must be replaced.
Aircraft Wiring.source: Nicked coaxial center conductor: replace the entire cable run

Aircraft Wiring.clue: When a wire harness outer jacket is damaged, the correct response depends on extent of damage: minor damage allows heat-shrink or conduit repair per the manual, but severe damage requires this.
choices: Application of vinyl tape over the damaged section
Installation of a metal shield over the damaged area
Aircraft Wiring.explanation: Severe outer jacket damage — especially if individual wire insulation is compromised — cannot be repaired to original airworthiness. The harness must be replaced.
Aircraft Wiring.source: Damaged harness jacket: assess damage and repair per manual or replace if severe

Aircraft Wiring.clue: Per AC 43.13-1B, wire bundles routed through pressurized areas must have this minimum clearance from flight control cables, hydraulic lines, and high-temperature ducting to prevent chafing, heat damage, and interference.
choices: No clearance required if a wrap of silicone tape is applied
A minimum separation distance specified in the aircraft's wiring diagram or IPC
One inch from all other aircraft systems regardless of installation
Contact is acceptable if anti-chafe conduit is installed over the bundle

Aircraft Wiring.clue: When routing a new wire bundle through an existing harness clamp, the clamp must not be overtightened to prevent this failure mode — which creates high-resistance connections and intermittent faults.
choices: Conductor crushing that deforms the wire and damages insulation
Electrolytic corrosion from moisture ingress at connector backshells

Aircraft Wiring.clue: The braided metal shield surrounding a sensitive signal wire in an avionics installation serves this primary purpose — and must be grounded per the installation drawing to be effective.
choices: Preventing external electromagnetic interference from coupling into the signal path
Providing a redundant ground return path for the signal current
Increasing the wire's current-carrying capacity by adding conductor area
Protecting the wire from physical abrasion during maintenance

Aircraft Wiring.clue: After installing a new comm radio, the pilot reports alternator whine in the headset that varies with engine RPM. The most likely cause is EMI entering the audio system through this path.
choices: The VHF antenna picking up alternator harmonics on the comm frequency
The headset jack's internal microphone preamp oscillating due to impedance mismatch
The comm radio's internal power supply filter capacitor failing open
The power supply wiring — inadequate filtering or a missing ground on the radio's power input

Tools & Test Equipment.clue: In an avionics repair shop, this hardened tool is struck with a hammer to create a guide indentation that prevents a drill bit from wandering on metal.
Tools & Test Equipment.explanation: The center punch creates a conical seat for the drill bit tip, preventing lateral movement (wandering) before the drill cuts through the metal surface.
Tools & Test Equipment.source: Center punch creates guide indentation to prevent drill wandering

Tools & Test Equipment.clue: This test instrument allows a technician to visualize voltage waveforms over time, observing signal amplitude, frequency, and shape on a calibrated display.
Tools & Test Equipment.explanation: An oscilloscope displays voltage vs. time, enabling measurement and visualization of amplitude, frequency, and waveform shape — information that a DMM cannot provide.
Tools & Test Equipment.source: Oscilloscope visualizes voltage waveforms showing amplitude, frequency, and shape

Tools & Test Equipment.clue: When a multimeter set to ohms displays 'OL', this is the most likely circuit condition being indicated.
choices: A perfectly balanced resistance equal to the meter's full-scale reading
A short circuit reading below the meter's minimum range
An open circuit or resistance exceeding the meter's selected range
A reverse-biased diode in the measurement path
Tools & Test Equipment.explanation: 'OL' means the resistance exceeds the meter's range — typically an open circuit (infinite resistance) or a legitimate high-resistance reading that exceeds the selected scale.
Tools & Test Equipment.source: OL on multimeter ohms = open circuit or out of range

Tools & Test Equipment.clue: This test instrument captures, displays, and decodes ARINC 429 bus traffic in real time, enabling a technician to verify correct data transmission and identify communication faults.
Tools & Test Equipment.explanation: A databus analyzer decodes the bus protocol layer — showing individual ARINC 429 labels, values, timing, and errors — unlike an oscilloscope which shows only the electrical waveform without decoding.
Tools & Test Equipment.source: Digital databus analyzer captures, displays, and decodes bus traffic to verify and diagnose

Tools & Test Equipment.clue: For capturing and evaluating an intermittent digital databus failure that occurs randomly during flight, this instrument is the most useful because it monitors continuously and records all bus events.
Tools & Test Equipment.explanation: A protocol/databus analyzer captures all bus traffic continuously, recording error events and their timestamps even for brief, intermittent failures — unlike an oscilloscope which requires triggering at the right moment.
Tools & Test Equipment.source: Databus analyzer is most useful for capturing intermittent digital bus failures

Tools & Test Equipment.clue: In avionics troubleshooting, this instrument captures real-time bus traffic, decodes data words to reveal their labels and values, and identifies transmissions that are incorrect or missing.
Tools & Test Equipment.explanation: A databus analyzer captures and decodes bus traffic in real time — showing each data word's label, value, and any error status — enabling direct identification of bad or missing transmissions.
Tools & Test Equipment.source: Databus analyzer captures real-time bus traffic and decodes data words

Tools & Test Equipment.clue: The standard specialized test instrument used in avionics maintenance to capture and analyze ARINC 429 and other digital databus traffic is called by this name.
Tools & Test Equipment.explanation: A protocol analyzer (databus analyzer) is the specialized instrument for digital bus traffic capture and analysis in avionics — connecting non-intrusively to the bus and decoding all protocol data.
Tools & Test Equipment.source: Databus/protocol analyzer is the tool for digital databus traffic analysis

Tools & Test Equipment.clue: To confirm that a broken wire is preventing a warning light from illuminating, a technician should perform this type of test with a multimeter, measuring resistance between the wire's endpoints.
choices: Voltage measurement with the circuit energized
Insulation resistance test with a megohmmeter
Current measurement in series with the warning light
Continuity test measuring resistance between wire ends
Tools & Test Equipment.explanation: A continuity test measures resistance between the two ends of a suspected broken wire. Near-zero ohms confirms an intact path; OL (infinite resistance) confirms the wire is broken.
Tools & Test Equipment.source: Continuity test with multimeter confirms broken wire using resistance measurement

Tools & Test Equipment.clue: The three steps for accurate micrometer measurement are: clean the measuring surfaces, apply consistent gentle pressure using the ratchet thimble, and do this before taking any reading.
choices: Zero the micrometer with the surfaces closed
Mark the measurement point with a scribe
Tools & Test Equipment.explanation: Zeroing the micrometer before use ensures the zero reference is correct. A micrometer that doesn't read zero when closed will have a constant offset error in every measurement.
Tools & Test Equipment.source: Micrometer use: clean, zero, apply gentle consistent pressure

Tools & Test Equipment.clue: The most important factor in preventing cam-out and screw head damage when using a manual screwdriver is using one that completely fills and does this to the screw head recess.
choices: Exceeds the torque specification of the fastener
Fully engages (fits snugly into) the screw head
Has a rubber-grip handle for maximum torque
Matches the color code of the screw head material
Tools & Test Equipment.explanation: Full engagement means the blade or bit fills the screw head recess completely — maximizing contact area, distributing force evenly, and preventing the bit from riding up and camming out.
Tools & Test Equipment.source: Screwdriver must fully engage the screw head to prevent cam-out

Tools & Test Equipment.clue: A multimeter measures these three electrical quantities in circuits, combining what were traditionally three separate instruments into one.
Tools & Test Equipment.explanation: A multimeter combines voltmeter (voltage), ammeter (current), and ohmmeter (resistance) functions in one instrument — hence 'multi' (multiple) meter.
Tools & Test Equipment.source: Multimeter measures voltage, current, and resistance

Shop Safety.clue: Federal regulations require these documents, which detail chemical hazards and emergency procedures, to be stored where every shop worker can reach them without delay.
In a GA repair shop, disposing of this type of material requires a licensed vendor and must never involve dumping it down a drain or in the regular trash.
An avionics tech drops a soldering iron that narrowly misses a coworker and lands harmlessly on the floor. This unplanned event with no injury but real danger potential has a specific safety term.
This is the first required action when a tech detects a burning electrical odor from an avionics rack during a bench test.
choices: Continue testing to isolate the source

Shop Safety.clue: These three steps — checking vent caps, confirming charge state, and using a non-conductive sealed container — must be completed before moving this type of aircraft battery.
Before connecting this high-voltage insulation resistance test instrument, two safety steps are mandatory: verify the circuit is de-energized and discharge all capacitors.
This document, required for every hazardous chemical in the shop, lists the substance's hazards, safe handling procedures, required PPE, and first-aid steps for exposure.
These three ground rules — avoid the rear, approach from the front, and wait for the pilot's signal — define the correct way to approach a running helicopter on the ramp.

These three steps — containing with absorbent materials, cutting power to nearby electronics, and following hazmat cleanup procedures — define the correct response to this type of bench-area contamination event.
choices: Hydraulic fluid spill near avionics equipment
An ESD event at the bench
A battery electrolyte spill on the tarmac
A fuel overflow during gravity fill

Shop Safety.clue: Cadmium's toxicity means these rechargeable aircraft batteries cannot be thrown in regular trash and must be handled through hazardous materials disposal channels.
Verifying this shop safety device involves checking the pressure gauge, confirming the pin and seal are intact, inspecting for damage, and ensuring the annual tag is current.
Reading this document for each chemical before first use gives a new technician the hazards, PPE requirements, and emergency steps needed for safe handling.
The correct sequence for helping an electrical shock victim: verify the area is safe, cut power before touching the person, then summon emergency help — in that order.
These self-closing, approved, labeled containers kept in a dedicated cabinet away from sparks are the OSHA-compliant way to store volatile cleaning solvents in an avionics shop.
This specified range — 6 to 8 per inch — defines how tightly to apply the double-twist method when installing safety wire on aviation fasteners.
choices: The minimum thread engagement for AN nuts
The maximum torque tolerance for AN bolts
The acceptable twist rate for safety wire
The pitch count for aviation-grade hex bolts

Shop Safety.clue: Leave the area, get fresh air, notify a supervisor, and file an incident report — these four steps define the correct response when a technician feels dizzy in this type of chemical exposure situation.

Counting tools at the start and end of every job and immediately retrieving any dropped hardware defines this practice, which is the most effective way to prevent foreign object damage in avionics work.
choices: Strict tool control for FOD prevention

Shop Safety.clue: A grounded anti-static wrist strap and a specially equipped handling station are the two required safeguards before touching any sensitive avionics component.
Stop work, find the item before closing the panels, and log the event — these are the required steps when this type of small fastener goes missing during avionics panel installation.

Before entering an aircraft fuel tank or other confined space for avionics wiring inspection, FAA and OSHA regulations require completing this critical step to ensure the atmosphere is safe for entry.
choices: Running the aircraft APU for ventilation for 30 minutes before entry
Testing the atmosphere with a calibrated gas detector for oxygen level, flammable vapors, and toxic gases
Spraying the space with a degreasing agent to neutralize residual fumes
Verifying the aircraft insurance covers confined-space maintenance

Shop Safety.clue: A technician performing soldering work on avionics circuit boards should use this specific type of ventilation to prevent inhalation of flux fumes — which contain rosin decomposition products that can cause occupational asthma.
choices: An open shop door providing general building ventilation
A standard dust mask rated for particulate filtration only
A fume extractor with activated carbon filter positioned at the soldering point
A full-face respirator with organic vapor cartridges rated for 8-hour use

Shop Safety.clue: During engine run-up while troubleshooting avionics near the flight line, this PPE item is required because sustained exposure to turbine engine noise levels (typically 130+ dB) causes this type of irreversible occupational injury.
choices: Impact-rated safety glasses to prevent FOD eye injuries
Steel-toed boots to protect against dropped tooling
High-visibility vest for ramp traffic awareness
Hearing protection (earplugs or earmuffs) to prevent permanent noise-induced hearing loss

Shop Safety.clue: This class of fire involves energized electrical equipment — common in avionics shops — and must be extinguished with CO₂ or dry chemical agents, never water.
The PASS technique — Pull the pin, Aim at the base of the fire, Squeeze the handle, Sweep side to side — is the correct operating procedure for this safety device.

When evacuating a hangar during a fire alarm, technicians must do this with any aircraft power and test equipment before leaving — failure to do so significantly increases the risk of the fire spreading.
choices: Move the test equipment to the nearest emergency exit for later retrieval
Cover sensitive avionics with anti-static bags to prevent smoke damage
Disconnect or de-energize all aircraft and bench power sources
Lock the tool cabinet to prevent unauthorized access during evacuation

Shop Safety.clue: A lithium battery in an avionics unit enters thermal runaway, producing white smoke, intense heat, and a chemical fire. Standard CO₂ and dry chemical extinguishers are ineffective because lithium fires require this response.
choices: Flooding the area with halon gas to reduce oxygen concentration below 12%
Applying a standard ABC dry chemical extinguisher at maximum discharge rate
Smothering the fire with a standard fire blanket rated for Class B use
Applying copious water to cool the cells below the thermal runaway threshold

Shop Safety.clue: Before beginning bench work on any avionics LRU, a technician's first action — before opening, testing, or troubleshooting — should be to verify this is available and current.
choices: The manufacturer's current Component Maintenance Manual (CMM)
The customer's purchase order and billing authorization
Last month's calibration log for all bench test equipment
The shop's current liability insurance certificate

Shop Safety.clue: A repair station's "Buy-Back" or bench check procedure — where a returned unit tests serviceable with no fault found — must still generate this documentation before the unit can be released back to the customer.
choices: A supplemental type certificate confirming the unit's configuration
A maintenance record entry with test results and a return-to-service statement
An FAA field approval letter authorizing the release
A manufacturer's service bulletin compliance record

Shop Safety.clue: When receiving a unit for repair, the incoming inspection must document its physical condition — and specifically check these items to establish accountability and prevent later disputes about pre-existing damage.
choices: The unit's retail replacement value and insurance coverage
The customer's maintenance training records and qualifications
External damage, missing hardware, and any non-standard modifications visible on the unit
The unit's electromagnetic emission profile measured at the shipping dock

Shop Safety.clue: A technician completes a repair and the unit passes all bench tests. Before the unit can be released with an 8130-3 tag, this person — whose authority is defined in the repair station manual — must review the work and approve the return to service.
choices: The original equipment manufacturer's technical support engineer
The FAA's principal avionics inspector assigned to the repair station
The repair station's designated inspector or quality control representative

Shop Safety.clue: Static discharge as low as this voltage level can destroy a CMOS gate inside an avionics LRU — far below what a person can feel (approximately 3,000 volts).

Before touching any circuit boards or static-sensitive components, a technician must verify that the resistance reading on their ESD wrist strap tester falls within this range — confirming the strap provides both static dissipation and personnel safety.
choices: Less than 1 ohm (direct short to ground)
Between 750 kΩ and 10 MΩ (controlled dissipation with current limiting)
Greater than 100 MΩ (high-impedance isolation from ground)
Exactly 0 ohms (zero resistance to facility ground)

Shop Safety.clue: When transporting an avionics LRU from the stockroom to the ESD-protected bench, the unit must remain inside this packaging material — which dissipates static charge rather than generating it.
choices: Static-dissipative or conductive ESD bag (pink or silver/black)
Foam peanuts in a sealed plastic bin

Shop Safety.clue: An ESD-damaged component may pass initial bench testing but fail prematurely in service. This type of ESD failure — where the damage weakens but does not immediately destroy the component — accounts for the majority of field ESD-related failures.
choices: Catastrophic failure detected during acceptance testing
Electromagnetic interference from adjacent installed equipment
Latent defect — degraded but functional until stress causes later failure

Shop Safety.clue: Before and after every maintenance task inside an aircraft, technicians must perform this accountability procedure — which is the single most effective method of preventing foreign object damage.
choices: A complete tool inventory count matching the tool control sheet
A visual sweep of the work area with a flashlight
A supervisor walkthrough signoff on the FOD prevention checklist
A borescope inspection of all accessible compartments

Shop Safety.clue: A technician drops a small screw inside an avionics bay behind the instrument panel. The required response per Part 145 shop procedures is this — regardless of how difficult retrieval may be.
choices: Note it in the maintenance record and release the aircraft with the known FOD
Stop work immediately and retrieve the item before continuing — do not close any panels until it is found
Continue working and retrieve it during the next scheduled inspection
Notify the customer and let them decide whether retrieval is necessary

Shop Safety.clue: FOD pouches, magnetic trays, and parts containers placed within arm's reach during aircraft maintenance serve this specific purpose in the FOD prevention program.
choices: Organizing parts by size for faster installation during reassembly
Protecting removed parts from ESD damage on the shop floor
Containing loose hardware and fasteners so they cannot fall into the aircraft structure
Labeling removed components for warranty tracking purposes

Shop Safety.clue: Safety wire clippings, stripped wire insulation, cable tie tails, and solder splashes are all examples of this category of FOD that is uniquely hazardous because individual pieces are small enough to be overlooked but can cause electrical shorts or mechanical jams.
choices: Process-generated debris — byproducts of the maintenance work itself

final.clue: When troubleshooting intermittent ARINC 429 communication loss, this should be the first step before suspecting a failed LRU.
choices: Replace the transmitting LRU with a known-good unit
Check wiring continuity and connector integrity at both ends
Reprogram the receiving LRU's ARINC label configuration
Install a bus signal repeater to boost signal strength
final.explanation: Intermittent faults are most commonly caused by loose connectors or wiring issues. Checking continuity and connector integrity at both ends is the correct first step per systematic troubleshooting.
final.source: Intermittent ARINC 429 loss: first check wiring and connector integrity
